unit.name: Airlock / Pass-Through Chamber System
unit.cfj: Control material and personnel transfer between classified zones without breaching pressure cascade or contamination boundary

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of override paths capable of defeating contamination-boundary protection
rows.imp_rat: Override paths that can defeat boundary protection are latent bypasses of the entire containment function; central to the CFJ and severe because a single misuse causes a full breach.
rows.sat_rat: Override paths are frequently retained for maintenance and often poorly controlled, so this need is weakly served. Reducing override paths does not affect operational timing materially. Identifying and closing every override path capable of defeating containment requires deep, scarce security-and-safety analysis. Auditing and removing override paths is engineering-labor intensive with little hardware cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a manual action bypasses required interlock state sequencing
rows.imp_rat: A manual action bypassing interlock sequencing defeats the primary safeguard; central to the CFJ and severe because it enables the worst breach through human override.
rows.sat_rat: Manual bypass of sequencing remains a frequent real-world contamination pathway, so this is weakly served. Enforcing sequence over manual action has negligible time impact. Constraining manual bypass while preserving needed manual control requires careful design judgement. Minimal added cost, mainly logic and access-control provisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that emergency release behavior creates an uncontrolled contamination-boundary breach
rows.imp_rat: Emergency release creating an uncontrolled breach undermines containment precisely when hazards are elevated; relevant to safe-state design and severe when triggered.
rows.sat_rat: Emergency release remains a chronic tension where containment is often sacrificed, so status quo behavior is only partially defined and inconsistent. Emergency release acts instantly by design, but reconciling speed with containment is an unresolved trade. Balancing egress safety against containment demands scarce cross-disciplinary judgement that is often inconsistently applied. Adding controlled emergency-release provisions carries moderate cost and is frequently under-specified.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of directional airflow reversal across the classified-zone boundary during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Directional airflow reversal across the boundary is a definitive cascade breach carrying contaminants; highly central with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Airflow reversal during door opening is a persistent, hard-to-fully-eliminate transient despite established cascade design. Directional airflow design is handled within normal engineering timelines. Airflow directionality across boundaries requires CFD or airflow-visualization expertise that is not universally available. Airflow studies add cost but are typically scoped into qualification budgets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that loss of power, control communication, or pressure input leaves the airlock in a cascade-breaching state
rows.imp_rat: A fail state that leaves the airlock cascade-breaching on power/control loss is a critical safe-state failure; central to the CFJ and severe because it defeats containment when least expected.
rows.sat_rat: Loss-of-power/pressure safe states are addressed in mature designs but coverage of all combined loss conditions is inconsistent, leaving meaningful residual risk. Fail-safe behavior on power/comm loss acts immediately once designed, with no operational delay. Specifying fail-safe states across all loss modes requires above-average failure-analysis expertise that is not universal. Fail-safe hardware (spring-return locks, UPS, safe defaults) adds moderate cost that is often value-engineered away.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an alarm reset permits transfer while the triggering breach remains unresolved
rows.imp_rat: An alarm reset permitting transfer while the breach persists lets contamination continue while masking the fault; central to breach handling and severe due to compounded undetected exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm-reset-permits-transfer is a recognized pitfall inconsistently guarded against, leaving a real gap. Preventing transfer on alarm reset until breach clears imposes minor delay. Distinguishing alarm acknowledgement from breach resolution requires deliberate design judgement often missed. No significant added cost beyond logic design.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of defined fault scenarios in which control logic fails to reach the specified safe state
rows.imp_rat: Fault scenarios where logic fails to reach safe state leave containment defeated during faults; central to fail-safe design and severe because protection is lost when most needed.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage of all defined fault scenarios reaching safe state is incomplete in typical designs, a recurring documented gap. Reaching safe state on fault is instantaneous once specified, with no time cost. Enumerating fault scenarios and their safe states demands systematic FMEA expertise that is scarce. Thorough fault analysis is labor-intensive and often truncated to save cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contamination ingress events into a higher-classified zone during a cascade breach
rows.imp_rat: Contamination ingress into a higher-classified zone during a breach is the exact harm the CFJ prevents; both relevance and severity are near maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Properly designed cascades and interlocks substantially prevent ingress, but transient breaches during door cycling remain a real recurring vulnerability. Cascade recovery and alarm response are near-real-time when instrumentation is present. Recognizing and responding to a breach still depends on trained operators reacting correctly. Pressure cascade design and monitoring represent a significant but standard capital investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of pressure reversal between classified zones during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Pressure reversal between zones is a direct cascade breach that drives contaminant flow the wrong direction; both centrality and consequence to the CFJ are high.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked doors and sustained differentials are mature practice, though transient reversals during door opening remain a known residual failure mode. Pressure reversal prevention is designed in up front and does not extend transfer timelines materially. Preventing reversal relies on well-understood interlock and cascade logic that competent designers can specify. Adequate differential margins add modest cost but are standard budget items.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of power, pressure-input, or control-communication loss conditions lacking a defined safe state
rows.imp_rat: Loss conditions lacking a defined safe state leave the airlock in an undefined, likely breaching state on failure; central to fail-safe design and severe because protection vanishes during the failure.
rows.sat_rat: Common loss conditions are handled but less common combinations frequently lack defined safe states, leaving residual exposure. Defined safe states on loss conditions act without delay once designed. Systematically covering power/pressure/comm loss combinations needs specialized failure-mode expertise. Redundancy and safe-default provisioning add moderate cost often minimized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that control logic permits transfer while zone pressure is outside acceptance limits
rows.imp_rat: Control logic permitting transfer while pressure is out of limits directly authorizes a breach; central logic defect with severe contamination consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-window permissives are common practice yet limit definitions and sensor validity gaps leave recurring exposure. Blocking transfer when pressure is out of limits is enforced in real time via permissives. Defining acceptance limits and wiring them into permissives is standard but requires correct limit-setting judgement. Uses existing pressure sensors and controller logic at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material transfers involving residual surface contamination entering a cleaner classified zone
rows.imp_rat: Residual surface contamination entering a cleaner zone defeats the transfer-control purpose; a real contamination event though localized to transferred items, keeping severity high but not maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Residual surface contamination entering cleaner zones is a persistent, hard-to-verify failure because cleaning efficacy is operator-dependent and rarely confirmed at point of transfer. Surface wipe-down and decontamination steps add meaningful time to each material transfer. Effective surface decontamination relies heavily on trained operator technique and diligence, which varies. Disinfectants, wipes and sporicides are recurring consumable costs but modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that opposing chamber doors are open simultaneously during entry
rows.imp_rat: Opposing chamber doors open simultaneously during entry directly opens a contamination channel; central interlock failure with severe cross-zone consequences.
rows.sat_rat: As with transfer, interlocks make simultaneous opposing-door opening during entry rare where they are functioning and not bypassed. Entry sequencing costs only the brief interlock delay. The interlock enforces the rule so entrants need little judgement to comply. Cost is negligible beyond the existing interlock system.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an invalid or stale pressure indication authorizes a transfer transition
rows.imp_rat: A stale/invalid pressure indication authorizing a transition means decisions rest on false state, silently defeating the interlock; central to reliable control with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Stale/invalid pressure signals are a known weak spot; many systems trust the last reading, so this need is poorly to moderately served. Rejecting stale indications can add brief validation delays that are generally acceptable. Signal validity checking and staleness detection require specialized control-systems expertise not always present. Implementing signal-validation logic adds engineering cost that is frequently skipped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that opposing chamber doors are open concurrently during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Opposing chamber doors open concurrently is the fundamental interlock breach directly eliminating the boundary; consequences are severe cross-zone contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Door interlocks are a mature, widely deployed control that reliably prevents concurrent opening in most installations. Interlocks add only seconds of enforced sequencing between opposing doors. Mechanical or electronic interlocks remove reliance on operator memory to prevent both doors opening. Interlock hardware is a modest, standard cost embedded in the chamber.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that both airlock boundary doors are open concurrently during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Both boundary doors open concurrently is the canonical interlock failure that voids the contamination boundary; the breach is immediate and can be catastrophic for the higher-classified zone.
rows.sat_rat: Validated interlocks are a mature, widely deployed practice that reliably enforces the never-both-open rule, though bypasses and interlock failures remain rare residual risks. Mechanical and electronic door interlocks act instantaneously to prevent concurrent opening, imposing no time penalty on the overseer's governance. Interlocks remove reliance on operator vigilance, so preventing dual-door opening requires little skill from participating personnel. Interlock hardware is a standard, amortized fixture of qualified airlocks with modest incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an authorized transfer procedure permits a contamination-boundary breach
rows.imp_rat: A procedure that itself permits a boundary breach is a systematic latent defect affecting every execution; relevance high, severity high due to repeated exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Validation and review catch many latent flaws, but subtle procedure-level breach pathways slip through and are only found after incidents. Procedure validation review is a lengthy up-front activity, not a per-transfer delay. Detecting that an approved procedure permits a contamination breach demands scarce senior QA and validation expertise. Thorough procedure risk assessment consumes considerable specialist labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of simultaneous opposing-door openings during transfers
rows.imp_rat: Simultaneous opposing-door openings are the direct interlock breach the airlock must prevent; frequency reduction is central with severe contamination consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Properly functioning interlocks make simultaneous opposing-door openings very rare, a well-solved status-quo strength. Mechanical/electronic interlocks act instantaneously to prevent both doors opening at once. Interlocks remove reliance on operator memory or discipline, greatly reducing skill demand. Interlock hardware is a standard, modest cost component of any compliant airlock.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of contaminant passage across the breached transfer boundary
rows.imp_rat: Duration of contaminant passage across a breached boundary determines total contaminant transfer; central to containment with severity tied to exposure length.
rows.sat_rat: Breach events are off-nominal and status-quo response is inconsistent, leaving variable passage durations. Once a boundary is breached, minimizing contaminant passage duration depends on how fast closure or isolation happens, which is the core issue. Rapid response depends on operator recognition and correct reaction under pressure. No major direct cost driver beyond existing containment infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an authorized transfer procedure permits loss of the required pressure cascade
rows.imp_rat: A procedure permitting cascade loss is a systematic defect embedded in operations; high relevance to the job with high consequence across repeated use.
rows.sat_rat: Standard qualification reduces obvious cascade-loss flaws, but edge-case sequences that permit loss still evade review with meaningful frequency. Cascade-preservation review happens during procedure approval rather than at transfer time. Spotting that a procedure allows cascade loss requires experienced validation and HVAC judgement. Detailed pressure-scenario analysis adds specialist review cost during procedure development.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specified differential cannot be maintained because expected leakage paths or transfer disturbances were omitted
rows.imp_rat: If expected leakage paths are omitted so the differential can't be maintained, the boundary silently fails under real conditions; a systemic design gap central to reliable containment.
rows.sat_rat: Leakage and disturbance factors are frequently underestimated, making unmet differentials a common real failure. Accounting for leakage and disturbances lengthens analysis somewhat but within normal schedules. Anticipating all leakage paths and transfer disturbances demands deep experience many teams lack. Omitted leakage assumptions cause costly post-commissioning remediation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reset or restart restores access before required boundary conditions are reestablished
rows.imp_rat: Reset/restart restoring access before boundary conditions are reestablished reopens the zone prematurely; central to safe recovery with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Reset/restart handling is a known weak area where premature access restoration occurs, so it is only moderately served. Blocking access on reset until conditions reestablish adds a short, accepted delay. Designing restart sequencing that reverifies boundary conditions requires careful state-machine expertise. Little hardware cost; primarily engineering effort in logic design.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that items enter a cleaner classified zone without verified transfer status
rows.imp_rat: Items entering a cleaner zone without verified transfer status bypass the control the airlock provides; central to controlled transfer with high contamination consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Status labeling helps, but unverified items entering cleaner zones remains a recurring lapse relying on human diligence. Verifying transfer status of items adds a checkpoint step but no major delay. Depends on personnel checking labels, cleaning status, or release paperwork correctly. Labeling and status-tag systems are inexpensive and standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that both zone-facing boundaries are open simultaneously during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Both zone-facing boundaries open simultaneously is the definitive interlock breach opening a direct path; central to the CFJ with severe cross-zone consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Mechanical and electronic interlocks are mature and reliably prevent simultaneous opening in nearly all cases. Interlocks prevent dual-door opening instantly with no time penalty. Door interlock logic is a standard, low-skill catalog feature. Interlocks are inexpensive standard equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that barrier release occurs before the required pressure differential is established
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a barrier before the pressure differential is established directly opens the zone without protection; this sequencing error is central and highly consequential.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-interlocked release is established in GMP airlocks, though sensor drift and setpoint ambiguity leave real residual gaps. Pressure-confirmation-before-release logic adds only a brief sensor-settling delay that is well accepted. Tying release permissives to pressure sensors is common but requires care in setpoint and hysteresis definition. Requires differential pressure sensors and integration, a modest recurring cost that is standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of pressure-separation loss between classified zones during transfer activity
rows.imp_rat: Duration of pressure-separation loss defines the contaminant-transfer exposure window; central to the CFJ with severity scaling by lapse length.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery timing is fairly consistent under normal operation but stretches unpredictably when loads or seals interfere. The duration of pressure-separation loss depends on door cycle and recovery time, which sequenced airlocks minimize but do not eliminate. Keeping loss brief relies on operators following timed sequences correctly. Faster recovery may require higher air-change rates and energy expenditure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the required pressure hierarchy between adjacent classified zones is specified in the wrong direction
rows.imp_rat: Specifying the pressure hierarchy in the wrong direction is a systemic design error that inverts protection for every transfer; less frequent to occur but catastrophic when it does.
rows.sat_rat: Direction reversal errors are a recognized and reviewed risk, but wrong-direction specs still occasionally survive to construction. Cascade direction is defined early and does not delay work. Getting hierarchy direction right depends on correctly reading zone classifications, an error-prone judgement point. A wrong-direction specification is cheap to fix on paper but expensive if built.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a subsequent barrier release occurs before the pressure cascade recovers
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a subsequent barrier before cascade recovery reopens the zone prematurely; central sequencing control with high but often recoverable consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery-before-next-release logic is less consistently specified than initial release interlocks, so it is served but with recurring gaps. Requiring cascade recovery before the next release imposes a wait that current practice does define but not always tightly. Judging adequate recovery timing demands understanding of cascade dynamics that is not always applied consistently. No major added cost beyond existing pressure instrumentation and controller logic.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of contamination spread from the affected chamber into cleaner zones
rows.imp_rat: Contamination spread from the affected chamber into cleaner zones is the direct escalation the boundary must contain; central to the CFJ with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Once contamination enters a chamber, cascade design limits but does not reliably prevent spread to cleaner zones. Speed of isolation influences spread but is a separate need. Containing spread requires trained response judgement during an abnormal event. Cleanup and product loss costs can be large but are consequences, not the driver.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that invalid zone or boundary status is treated as permissive
rows.imp_rat: Treating invalid status as permissive is a fail-permissive logic defect that lets transfers proceed without real assurance; central and severe due to undetected breach potential.
rows.sat_rat: Default-to-safe on invalid status is understood best practice but inconsistently implemented, leaving real gaps. Treating invalid status as non-permissive is a logic rule with no time penalty once designed. Designing fail-not-permissive handling of invalid states needs deliberate defensive-design skill that is uneven. Marginal added logic cost, but validation infrastructure can add expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a boundary breach producing no discernible indication during transfer
rows.imp_rat: A breach producing no discernible indication means contamination proceeds undetected; relevant to detection reliability and severe due to silent, unremediated exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Many breaches produce no clear signal in status-quo systems, so silent breaches remain a genuine unmet risk. The concern is undetected breaches, not the time to detect. Noticing an indication-less breach depends on operator alertness with no instrument backup. Adding breach-detection instrumentation carries cost that is often deferred.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that opposing airlock boundaries open concurrently during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent opening of opposing boundaries is the primary interlock failure the airlock must prevent; the resulting open channel makes severity high.
rows.sat_rat: This is essentially the same well-proven exclusion pattern as concurrent-open prevention, so it is reliably handled by mature practice. Opposing-boundary exclusion is enforced in real time by control logic without added delay. Specifying mutual exclusion for opposing boundaries is standard interlock practice needing modest expertise. Reuses the same low-cost interlock hardware and logic already deployed for door pairs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of air crossing from the lower-classified zone into the higher-classified zone during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Air crossing from lower to higher classification is precisely the contamination event the boundary must stop; direct realization of the failure the CFJ guards against.
rows.sat_rat: Directional airflow with pressure cascade is a proven design that limits back-flow, though transient breaches during door cycling remain a known residual gap. Pressure cascade recovery introduces short dwell delays that established transfer sequences already accommodate. Maintaining airflow direction depends on qualified HVAC design more than operator skill once set up. Sustaining pressure cascades consumes continuous HVAC energy and filter costs that are accepted as baseline expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel or material entering a classified zone without confirmed release status
rows.imp_rat: Entry without confirmed release status lets unverified material/personnel into a classified zone; central to controlled transfer with severe contamination potential.
rows.sat_rat: Manual release verification is error-prone and inconsistently enforced, allowing unreleased items or people to enter under time pressure. Release-status checks add procedural time before entry. Confirming release status depends on operators consulting labels, logs or systems correctly. Labeling and status-tracking systems are low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of airborne contaminant escape from the chamber during opening of a zone-facing boundary
rows.imp_rat: Airborne contaminant escape when a zone-facing boundary opens directly transfers contamination out of the chamber; central to the CFJ with high consequence for adjacent zones.
rows.sat_rat: Escape during the brief door-open window remains an incompletely controlled transient despite cascade design. Contaminant-escape control at door opening is designed in and does not add transfer delay. Controlling escape during boundary opening needs specialized airflow and containment expertise. Overspill mitigation adds cost through airflow provisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of dismissing a pressure-cascade reversal as normal fluctuation
rows.imp_rat: Dismissing a cascade reversal as normal fluctuation lets a real breach go unaddressed; relevant to detection with severe consequence because the breach continues unrecognized.
rows.sat_rat: Without smart alarming, operators frequently rationalize genuine reversals as normal noise, a recurring error. Recognition speed matters but the core failure is misjudgment, not delay. Distinguishing a true cascade reversal from normal fluctuation demands experienced interpretation of noisy data. No major cost dimension.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that opposing access barriers are open simultaneously during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Simultaneous opening of opposing barriers directly defeats the airlock's core containment function, creating a direct open path between zones; consequences are severe cross-contamination and pressure-cascade loss.
rows.sat_rat: Door interlocking is a mature, widely deployed engineering control that reliably prevents simultaneous opening except during rare bypass or failure states. Mechanical and electronic interlocks act instantly to prevent both doors opening, so no time penalty is imposed on the overseer. Interlocks remove the reliance on operator judgement to avoid dual-door opening, though override and bypass conditions still demand vigilance. Interlock hardware is standard and inexpensive relative to the contamination risk it eliminates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that both airlock barriers are open concurrently during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent barrier opening is central to the CFJ since it eliminates the separation the airlock exists to provide; severity is high due to unmitigated contamination transfer.
rows.sat_rat: Simple mechanical/electrical interlocks preventing both doors open at once are mature, widely validated, and rarely fail, making this among the best-served needs. Standard two-door interlock logic prevents concurrent open states instantaneously with no time penalty once specified. Interlock design for mutually exclusive door opening is a textbook, well-understood competency requiring little scarce expertise. Hardware and PLC interlocks for door exclusion are inexpensive and universally deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a transfer beginning while either adjacent zone is outside its required classification state
rows.imp_rat: Beginning a transfer while an adjacent zone is out of classification state means transferring into/out of a non-conforming environment; central to controlled transfer with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Zone-state confirmation before transfer is inconsistently interlocked, so transfers sometimes begin before full classification is assured. Confirming both zones are in classification state before transfer can require waiting for recovery. Judging zone readiness relies on operator interpretation of environmental indicators. Monitoring instrumentation is standard cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible fault states lacking defined boundary-safe behavior
rows.imp_rat: Credible fault states lacking defined boundary-safe behavior create undefended failure modes; relevant to fail-safe completeness and severe because such faults breach containment unhandled.
rows.sat_rat: Undefined-behavior fault states are a persistent gap because analyses rarely enumerate every credible case. Defined boundary-safe behavior triggers immediately, no time impact. Finding credible fault states lacking defined behavior requires rigorous hazard analysis skill in short supply. Comprehensive fault-coverage analysis adds analytical cost commonly curtailed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of airflow reversals toward the higher-classification zone during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Airflow reversal toward the higher-classification zone is a core cascade breach; frequency reduction is central though a brief reversal may be partially recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked cascades and DP monitors substantially prevent reversals in mature installs, though transient reversals during door cycling still occur. Pressure-differential monitoring gives near-instant readback so timing is rarely the limiting factor for detecting reversals. Interpreting subtle airflow reversals still leans on operator vigilance and understanding of cascade behavior beyond simple gauge reading. HVAC and differential-pressure instrumentation carry meaningful capital and energy cost but are standard infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of mistaking incomplete chamber closure for complete closure
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking incomplete closure for complete closure lets the interlock proceed on a false-closed door; central detection defect with severe consequence akin to a concurrent-opening breach.
rows.sat_rat: Position sensors reduce but do not eliminate mistaking partial closure for full seal, especially with obstructions. Not primarily about speed. Detecting incomplete closure often relies on operator sight and feel rather than positive proof. Sensors are cheap; the gap is coverage, not cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of concurrent-opening events at the transfer interface not identified as boundary breaches
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent-opening events not identified as breaches means the worst interlock failure goes unrecognized; relevant to detection with severe consequence due to unremediated exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Many airlocks lack event logging that classifies concurrent openings as breaches, so such events go unrecorded and unrecognized. Detection of concurrent-opening events is instantaneous where logging interlocks exist. Recognizing an unlogged concurrent opening as a breach requires analytical review skill. Event-logging capability adds modest system cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that control logic enables opposing access points simultaneously
rows.imp_rat: Control logic that enables opposing access points simultaneously is the root-cause defect behind the worst interlock breach; highly central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Logic-level exclusion of opposing access is mature and reliably implemented, closely mirroring the top-served needs. Preventing simultaneous access enablement is enforced in logic without time cost. This is a standard mutual-exclusion requirement well within routine control-design skill. No added cost beyond existing interlock logic already present.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that loss of the required pressure relationship between classified zones is not identified as a boundary breach
rows.imp_rat: Failing to identify loss of the pressure relationship as a breach means the containment failure goes unrecognized and unremediated; central to the CFJ and severe due to undetected exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous DP monitoring with alarms makes undetected loss uncommon, though alarm setpoint and nuisance-alarm issues can mask genuine breaches. Pressure sensors and alarms flag loss of the required relationship in near real time. Interpreting whether an alarm truly constitutes a boundary breach still requires trained judgement. Magnehelic gauges and differential pressure monitoring are standard, low-cost installations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a door treated as closed remaining unsecured before opposing-door release
rows.imp_rat: A door treated as closed while actually unsecured before opposing release enables a concealed concurrent breach; central to interlock integrity with severe consequence because the barrier is falsely trusted.
rows.sat_rat: Position switches usually verify closure before release, but a switch-satisfied yet unsealed door remains an occasional failure mode. Not a timing issue. Confirming a door is truly secured before opposing release depends partly on operator confirmation. Position sensing is inexpensive and standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of pressure-cascade deviation from the required zone relationship during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Pressure-cascade deviation from the required relationship directly undermines the boundary-preserving mechanism; deviations can allow contaminant migration though often recoverable, tempering severity slightly.
rows.sat_rat: Modern airlocks with pressure sensors and interlocks make gross cascade deviations uncommon, though transient dips during door cycling still occur. Pressure recovery cycles impose short waits between door operations but timing is generally handled by the interlock system automatically. Maintaining the cascade depends heavily on trained operators recognizing gauge readings and not defeating interlocks, which is skill-dependent. HVAC and differential-pressure infrastructure is a fixed capital cost already sunk into the facility.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of next-door opening before chamber pressure stabilizes
rows.imp_rat: Opening the next door before chamber pressure stabilizes releases into an unrecovered state; a central sequencing error with high consequence for the receiving zone.
rows.sat_rat: Many systems key next-door release to time delay rather than actual pressure confirmation, leaving a recurring gap. Stabilization delay is built into interlock logic but adds waiting time before next-door release. Automated pressure-stabilization gating removes the need for operator judgement about readiness. Pressure sensing and timing logic are inexpensive relative to overall system cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of confirming readiness with a chamber door not fully closed and sealed
rows.imp_rat: Confirming readiness with a door not fully sealed authorizes transfer over a compromised boundary; central readiness-detection defect with severe consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks tied to door sensors make this fairly reliable, though seal-versus-position ambiguity persists. Readiness decision is quick; speed is not the loss. Interlock logic reduces the operator judgement needed to confirm door state at readiness. Standard sensor cost, already incurred.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of boundary crossings made while the chamber pressure state is outside acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Crossings while chamber pressure is outside acceptance directly move material/personnel through an unprotected boundary state; central to controlled transfer with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks tied to pressure state help, but crossings during marginal or drifting pressure still happen when overrides or manual judgement intervene. Waiting for acceptable pressure state before crossing adds minor delay tied to recovery time. Operators must read and interpret pressure indicators, leaving room for misjudgement under time pressure. No significant material cost beyond monitoring instrumentation already present.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of releasing a transfer into the receiving zone before chamber conditions recover to approved limits
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a transfer before chamber conditions recover exposes the receiving zone to unrestored conditions; central sequencing gate with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Premature release happens when recovery is judged by feel rather than confirmed measurement, a recurring gap. Waiting for chamber recovery before release imposes a dwell time that is sometimes shortcut under pressure. Deciding recovery is complete depends on operator reading of indicators or timers. Timers and indicators are inexpensive controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which the required minimum pressure differential falls below the boundary-preserving threshold during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Falling below the boundary-preserving differential threshold directly weakens the barrier; central to maintaining separation with high consequence when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Threshold specification is well-codified, though real-world drift below thresholds during transfer still occurs. Setting minimum differential thresholds is a fast specification step. Standard guidance (ISO, EU GMP Annex gives designers clear differential targets requiring modest skill. Maintaining minimum differentials imposes routine energy cost but nothing exceptional.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of contamination carried into the cleaner zone by personnel during passage
rows.imp_rat: Contamination carried into the cleaner zone by personnel is a direct realization of transfer failure; relevant to controlled personnel transfer with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Personnel remain the dominant contamination vector and outcomes vary widely with individual behavior. Gowning and personnel cleaning add time but that is not the core loss. Preventing personnel-borne contamination depends heavily on gowning technique, discipline, and behavioral training. Gowning consumables are a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unauthorized interlock overrides during chamber access
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized interlock overrides defeat the primary safeguard against concurrent opening; relevant to interlock integrity and severe because each override risks a full breach.
rows.sat_rat: Keyed/credentialed override controls reduce unauthorized use but discipline gaps persist. Not a speed issue. Preventing unauthorized overrides depends on procedural discipline and access control awareness. Access-control hardware is a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of passage beginning while the receiving zone is outside classification limits
rows.imp_rat: Passage beginning while the receiving zone is out of classification limits moves material into a non-conforming environment; relevant to controlled transfer with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Receiving-zone state is often not interlocked to the transfer, so passage into an out-of-limit zone remains a real gap. Not a speed issue. Verifying receiving-zone classification before passage may depend on operator awareness of adjacent-zone state. Cross-zone monitoring adds some instrumentation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of chamber contamination exceeding the release limit after transfer
rows.imp_rat: Chamber contamination exceeding release limit after transfer risks carrying contamination onward; relevant to material transfer control with high consequence for the cleaner zone.
rows.sat_rat: Without inline monitoring most systems infer cleanliness rather than confirm it, leaving real uncertainty about exceeding limits. Contamination testing turnaround can lag, but the core need is consistency not speed. Judging whether release limits are met requires trained sampling and interpretation. Sampling and analysis add recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of leakage through damaged chamber seals or incomplete closures during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Leakage through damaged seals or incomplete closures degrades the physical boundary; relevant to containment integrity with moderate-high consequence depending on leak magnitude.
rows.sat_rat: Seal leakage is a recurring silent failure mode because integrity is rarely verified at the moment of each transfer. Seal inspection is periodic rather than per-transfer, so degradation can persist unseen for a time. Detecting damaged seals or incomplete closure requires attentive inspection and maintenance competence. Gaskets and seals are cheap to replace but often deferred until failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unauthorized material or personnel crossings of a classified-zone boundary
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized boundary crossings compromise controlled transfer, part of the CFJ; severity depends on what crosses, so moderate-high rather than maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Procedural authorization plus some access hardware catches most unauthorized crossings, yet manual bypass and tailgating leave recurring gaps. Access-control and authorization checks add minor delay but are integrated into transfer workflows. Enforcement leans on personnel discipline and supervisor oversight rather than automated identity gating in many facilities. Badge readers and access controls carry moderate installed cost but are increasingly standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between detecting a contamination-boundary breach and stopping affected transfers
rows.imp_rat: Time to stop affected transfers after detecting a breach governs how long contamination continues; relevant to breach response and severe because delay multiplies exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Response is reasonably reliable where alarms and clear stop procedures exist, though communication delays occur. Time from detection to stopping affected transfers depends on alarm response procedures that are usually prompt. Deciding to halt transfers requires trained escalation judgement. Stopping transfers costs production time but procedures accept it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a transfer begins while baseline differential pressure is outside validated limits
rows.imp_rat: Beginning a transfer with baseline differential outside validated limits starts from an unprotected state; central readiness gate with high consequence for the transfer.
rows.sat_rat: Where interlocks tie start to validated pressure it is well controlled, but many airlocks rely on the operator glancing at a gauge, leaving room for missed excursions. Magnehelic gauges or transmitters show baseline pressure instantly before a transfer. Operators must know and check the correct limits, introducing some reliance on training. Pressure indication is standard installed instrumentation with low marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of contamination carried into the cleaner zone on material exteriors
rows.imp_rat: Contamination carried into the cleaner zone on material exteriors is a direct transfer-control failure; relevant to material transfer with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Manual exterior sanitization is inconsistent in coverage, leaving recurring carryover into cleaner zones. Surface sanitization of material exteriors adds handling time but timing isn't the core issue. Thorough exterior wipe-down depends on operator technique and coverage. Wipes and disinfectants are ongoing consumable costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of personnel transfers across the contamination boundary involving compromised gowning
rows.imp_rat: Compromised gowning during personnel transfer introduces contamination across the boundary; relevant to controlled personnel transfer but one contributing factor among several, keeping proxies moderate-high.
rows.sat_rat: Compromised gowning is a leading contamination route that current visual checks catch inconsistently, leaving real recurring gaps. Gowning verification adds time but is built into established entry routines. Correct gowning integrity depends almost entirely on personnel training, discipline and periodic requalification, making it a chronic skill-dependent vulnerability. Gowns and gowning qualification programs are ongoing costs accepted as necessary.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of entrants admitted before the required chamber pressure state is restored
rows.imp_rat: Admitting entrants before chamber pressure is restored exposes the receiving zone to an unrecovered state; central sequencing control with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-linked interlocks reduce premature admission but manual overrides and marginal states leave recurring gaps. Waiting for pressure restoration before admitting entrants adds enforced delay. Depends on operator not admitting prematurely, a vigilance-dependent behavior. No meaningful cost dimension beyond monitoring.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of viable contamination remaining on transfer-contact surfaces after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Viable contamination remaining on transfer-contact surfaces after cleaning defeats decontamination; relevant to controlled transfer with high consequence for the cleaner zone.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning reduces viable contamination well but residual survivors persist without inline verification. Contact time for cleaning is prescribed, so speed is secondary. Achieving viable-contaminant kill depends on correct agent, contact time, and technique. Validated disinfectants and consumables are recurring expenses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual chamber contamination transferred to materials or personnel during passage
rows.imp_rat: Residual chamber contamination transferred to materials/personnel defeats the cleaning purpose of the pass-through; relevant to controlled transfer with high but item-scoped consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning practices reduce but never fully eliminate residual carryover, a persistent recurring gap. Residual contamination transfer is not primarily a timing problem. Effective decon and wipe-down before passage depends on operator technique and thoroughness. Consumables and decontaminant are ongoing but modest costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transfers proceeding with required decontamination or sanitization steps incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Proceeding with incomplete decon/sanitization allows residual contamination to cross; relevant to controlled transfer with high consequence, though a process-adherence issue.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures specify decon steps but incomplete or skipped sanitization is a common real-world lapse the overseer only detects retrospectively. Decon steps have defined dwell times but enforcing completion before proceeding adds no automation-driven delay. Ensuring decon completeness relies heavily on operator adherence and supervisory checks. Decon materials and dwell time are routine consumables with predictable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of interface seal-integrity losses not identified during material or personnel transfers
rows.imp_rat: Seal-integrity losses not identified during transfers mean a degraded boundary goes unnoticed; relevant to detection with high consequence due to undetected leakage.
rows.sat_rat: Interface seal failures during transfers frequently go unidentified because there is no per-transfer integrity check. Seal-integrity loss is not detected in real time, delaying identification until scheduled checks. Identifying integrity loss during a transfer demands inspection competence rarely applied per-event. Continuous seal monitoring is uncommon due to installation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of pressure differential outside approved limits between classified zones during a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Duration of differential outside approved limits governs the exposure window for cascade breach; central to the CFJ with severity tied to lapse length.
rows.sat_rat: Automated pressure control typically restores limits promptly, though excursion duration is not always captured or trended. Time spent outside approved differential limits is bounded by HVAC recovery response, which is reasonably quick. Little operator skill is needed once the automated pressure control is qualified. HVAC responsiveness carries energy cost but is part of accepted operating expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfers authorized using pressure limits inconsistent with the applicable classified zones
rows.imp_rat: Authorizing transfers with pressure limits inconsistent with the zones applies wrong criteria to the boundary decision; relevant defect with high consequence for permitted breaches.
rows.sat_rat: Validated setpoints usually align with zones, but mismatches from changes or transcription errors still authorize transfers on wrong limits occasionally. Setting correct pressure limits is a one-time configuration, not a per-transfer delay. Matching limits to zone classification requires qualified engineering knowledge. Configuration is inexpensive once validated.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of pressure differential below the required threshold during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Duration of differential below threshold governs exposure window for contaminant migration; central to the CFJ, severity scaled by how long the lapse persists.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks generally block progression until threshold is restored, so sub-threshold intervals are usually contained but not eliminated. Recovery to threshold pressure after door cycling is bounded by HVAC response time, which is often slower than desired during rapid transfers. Operators rely on displayed pressure and interlock timing rather than judgement, limiting skill dependence. Faster pressure recovery demands higher HVAC capacity and energy, a real recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of detected pressure excursions remaining unaddressed during transfer activities
rows.imp_rat: Detected excursions left unaddressed prolong the breach window; relevant to breach response with high consequence tied to unresolved duration.
rows.sat_rat: Local alarms exist but the duration an excursion stays unaddressed varies widely depending on staffing and monitoring coverage. Alarm response time depends on staff availability and can lag when unattended. Deciding whether an excursion is actionable requires trained judgement. Alarming and monitoring add moderate ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evaluated control logic differs from approved logic for the designated airlock or pass-through chamber
rows.imp_rat: Evaluating logic that differs from approved logic means the operating control is unverified; relevant to assurance and severe because unqualified logic may contain breaching defects.
rows.sat_rat: Logic version mismatches occur when change control is weak, so this is moderately served with recurring gaps. Verifying evaluated-versus-approved logic match is a review activity with modest time cost. Configuration verification requires disciplined change-control skill that is often uneven. Version-control tooling and reviews add moderate administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of containment actions redistributing contamination beyond the affected chamber
rows.imp_rat: Containment actions redistributing contamination beyond the affected chamber worsen a breach during response; relevant to breach handling with high consequence for spread.
rows.sat_rat: Containment response is procedure-dependent and inconsistently prevents secondary spread. Speed matters in response but the core is doing it correctly. Executing containment without spreading contamination demands trained judgement during a stressful abnormal event. Cleanup costs are consequences, not the driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of contact between personnel or transferred materials and lower-classified surfaces while crossing the boundary
rows.imp_rat: Contact with lower-classified surfaces during crossing introduces contamination but is a lesser, more localized contributor; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Incidental contact during boundary crossing is common, largely unmonitored, and heavily behavior-driven, so outcomes vary widely. Avoiding contact with lower-classified surfaces adds careful, slower movement during crossing. Preventing inadvertent contact depends on operator technique, spatial awareness and behavioral discipline that are difficult to enforce. Contact-minimizing fixtures and staging exist at modest cost but are not universal.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual contamination remaining inside the chamber after a transfer
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination remaining inside the chamber after transfer can affect subsequent cycles; relevant to sustained control with moderate consequence unless it reaches a cleaner zone.
rows.sat_rat: Residual contamination inside the chamber is inconsistently removed and seldom verified, leaving variable outcomes. Post-transfer cleaning of the chamber adds turnaround time. Thorough residual-contamination removal depends on operator cleaning technique and consistency. Cleaning agents and labor are recurring modest costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of items released before required surface decontamination is complete
rows.imp_rat: Releasing items before required surface decon is complete lets contamination pass as clean; relevant to material transfer control with high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Without gating, items released before decon completion is a recurring lapse dependent on operator memory. Ensuring decon completion before release enforces the full contact time. Depends on operator tracking which items are finished, error-prone under load. Premature release causes rework cost but the control is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between occurrence of a boundary breach and classification of the event as a breach
rows.imp_rat: Delay in classifying an event as a breach postpones remediation and prolongs exposure; relevant to breach handling with high consequence tied to detection lag.
rows.sat_rat: Breach classification is inconsistent and delayed under current manual review practices, extending exposure. Classifying an event as a breach often lags because it awaits review meetings or QA disposition. Correct classification requires experienced quality judgement of ambiguous evidence. Investigation effort consumes QA labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the protocol accepts a chamber cycle with post-cycle residues above release criteria
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a cycle with residues above release criteria lets contamination pass as clean; relevant to material transfer control and severe because contaminated items reach cleaner zones.
rows.sat_rat: Mature bio-decontamination validation with biological and chemical indicators substantially reduces false accepts, though residue detection sensitivity and sampling gaps still let some pass. Release decisions wait on analytical residue results that are routinely turned around within validated timelines, so time is not the binding constraint. Setting defensible release criteria demands scarce microbiological and toxicological judgement, and misjudged limits are a recurring source of false accepts. Re-testing and batch rejection when a bad accept surfaces later carry heavy cost, but that cost is downstream of the reliability failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminant migration through unintended air paths around the chamber boundary
rows.imp_rat: Contaminant migration through unintended air paths around the boundary is a bypass of the intended barrier; relevant to containment integrity with high consequence when leakage paths exist.
rows.sat_rat: Flanking leakage around the chamber boundary is a well-known but not always fully closed contamination route. Identifying unintended air paths is part of standard design review timing. Finding bypass leakage around chamber-to-wall interfaces demands careful, experienced inspection. Sealing unintended paths is inexpensive if caught during install.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credible cascade-breaching transitions excluded from acceptance evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Excluding credible cascade-breaching transitions from acceptance evaluation leaves failure modes unqualified; relevant to assurance completeness with high consequence for unassessed breaches.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance evaluations routinely omit some breaching transitions, so coverage gaps persist. Broadening acceptance evaluation coverage is an upfront effort, not a runtime factor. Enumerating all credible cascade-breaching transitions for evaluation requires strong analytical rigor. More extensive evaluation raises verification cost that is often constrained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaged material packaging releasing contaminants during passage
rows.imp_rat: Damaged packaging releasing contaminants during passage introduces contamination into the chamber; relevant to material transfer with high consequence for the receiving zone.
rows.sat_rat: Reliance on manual inspection makes packaging-damage release an inconsistently caught hazard. Not time-driven. Detecting damaged packaging depends on operator visual inspection skill. Rejected material and rework carry cost consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a chamber door remaining incompletely closed or unsecured after transfer
rows.imp_rat: A door remaining incompletely closed after transfer leaves a standing boundary gap; relevant to containment integrity with high consequence until detected.
rows.sat_rat: Auto-close and status indication handle most cases, but residual incomplete-closure events still occur. Not a time-dominated need. Ensuring a door fully secures after transfer relies on auto-close plus operator check. Auto-closers and sensors are modest standard costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of destabilization in adjacent room pressure cascades during chamber operation
rows.imp_rat: Destabilizing adjacent room cascades during chamber operation can propagate pressure imbalance; relevant to system-wide cascade integrity with moderate, usually transient consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Adjacent cascade disturbance during chamber operation is a common commissioning issue not always fully resolved. Cascade stability during operation is assessed during commissioning without major schedule impact. Predicting cascade destabilization from chamber cycling needs advanced HVAC dynamics expertise. Destabilization fixes such as fast-response controls carry real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of airlock state commands conflicting with facility pressure-cascade requirements
rows.imp_rat: State commands conflicting with facility cascade requirements can drive the airlock against the intended pressure regime; relevant design/logic defect with high consequence if executed.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts between airlock commands and facility cascade are common where integration is weak, giving moderate service. Command-cascade conflict avoidance is a design-time concern with no runtime delay. Aligning airlock commands with facility cascade requirements demands cross-system coordination skill. Coordination effort adds engineering cost but little hardware.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of item surface samples exceeding contamination acceptance criteria before cleaner-zone entry
rows.imp_rat: Surface samples exceeding acceptance before cleaner-zone entry indicate inadequate decon; relevant to material transfer verification with moderate consequence, a detection safeguard.
rows.sat_rat: Routine per-transfer surface sampling is rarely done, so exceedances are usually detected only in periodic monitoring. Surface sampling, when done, adds significant lab turnaround time. Sampling and interpretation require trained personnel. Sampling and analysis consume measurable lab resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a transfer load obstructs complete closure of a chamber access barrier
rows.imp_rat: A transfer load obstructing complete closure of a barrier prevents sealing; relevant to containment integrity with high consequence if a door cannot close.
rows.sat_rat: Load-obstructed closures still occur because chamber sizing and loading are operator-managed without hard safeguards. Discovering an obstruction to closure happens at the door, adding immediate delay. Preventing load obstruction depends on operator planning and awareness of chamber capacity. No significant cost beyond staging discipline.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of pressure differentials falling outside specified limits during expected facility fluctuations
rows.imp_rat: Differentials falling outside limits during expected fluctuations shows the cascade lacks robustness margin; relevant to boundary maintenance with moderate, usually transient consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Differential excursions during facility fluctuations remain a fairly common operational occurrence despite monitoring. Fluctuation tolerance is designed in and verified during commissioning within schedule. Ensuring differentials hold through fluctuations needs control-tuning expertise that varies by team. Robust control responses add energy and equipment cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contamination crosses the closed chamber boundary during transfer-related pressure changes
rows.imp_rat: Contamination crossing the closed boundary during pressure changes is a subtle transient breach; relevant to containment integrity with high consequence during transitions.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked doors and pressure-cascade design are mature and largely prevent boundary crossing, though dynamic transient behavior during transfer remains a residual risk. Pressure transients during transfer happen fast, and monitoring keeps pace, so time is not the limiting factor. Diagnosing and preventing ingress during pressure changes requires cascade-design expertise that is specialized but generally available in the design phase. Breaches force line stoppages and investigations at high cost, but the loss is fundamentally a contamination-control failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of differential pressure crossing validated limits during transfers
rows.imp_rat: Differential crossing validated limits during transfers indicates transient cascade instability; relevant to boundary maintenance but often brief and recoverable, moderating severity.
rows.sat_rat: Well-balanced systems limit excursions, but transient limit crossings during transfers are common and not always logged or addressed. Pressure fluctuation during door cycling resolves in seconds with proper HVAC recovery. Little operator skill is needed since recovery is a designed HVAC response. Robust recovery depends on adequate air-handling capacity, a designed capital cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of adjacent-zone classifications or pressure baselines identified incorrectly in the pressure-cascade requirements
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly identified adjacent-zone classifications or baselines in the cascade requirements corrupt the design basis; relevant to correct specification with high consequence if wrong values drive the cascade.
rows.sat_rat: Incorrect classification or baseline inputs are a known error source, partly caught by review but not eliminated. Verifying classifications and baselines is a bounded review activity. Correctly identifying adjacent classifications relies on accurate facility documentation and careful reading. Misidentified baselines are cheap to fix on paper, costly if built.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of item surfaces becoming recontaminated after decontamination and before cleaner-zone entry
rows.imp_rat: Recontamination after decon and before cleaner-zone entry negates the cleaning step; relevant to controlled transfer with moderate-high consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Recontamination between steps is a common, hard-to-detect failure with few dedicated controls. Minimizing dwell between decon and entry requires prompt handling adding time pressure. Avoiding recontamination depends on careful handling technique and surface awareness. Little direct cost beyond re-cleaning waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transfers with validated steps omitted or performed out of sequence
rows.imp_rat: Omitted or out-of-sequence validated steps weaken process assurance and may allow contamination; relevant but a procedural-adherence issue with variable consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Without forced step-gating, omitted or out-of-sequence steps recur and are typically caught only in later record review. Sequence enforcement via checklists adds little time but manual verification is not instantaneous. Correct sequencing depends on trained operators following SOPs without electronic step-gating in most airlocks. Paper checklists are cheap; electronic batch-record enforcement is costlier and less common at airlocks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between specified chamber pressure relationships and the intended room pressure cascade
rows.imp_rat: A mismatch between specified chamber and intended room cascade is a design/spec defect that can propagate contamination if uncaught; it is one step removed from live operation, moderating relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Mature design-review and P&ID cross-checking practices catch most cascade mismatches, yet coordination errors between chamber vendor and facility designer still slip through. URS and pressure-cascade specification cycles are well-established so aligning chamber relationships to the cascade rarely adds significant delay. Experienced HVAC and cleanroom design engineers routinely map chamber differentials to room cascades, though it still leans on scarce specialist judgement. Mismatches caught late trigger costly rework, but early URS review usually contains this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a contamination-boundary breach goes undetected during transfer qualification
rows.imp_rat: A breach undetected during qualification means the boundary weakness ships into operation; relevant to assurance with high consequence because it undermines confidence in containment.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification protocols catch many breach modes but can miss subtle or dynamic breaches not represented in the test design. Qualification testing is a scheduled activity, not time-critical during routine use. Detecting a breach during qualification requires specialized study design and analytical expertise. Smoke studies and challenge testing carry consultant and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of interlock override activation beyond the approved activity
rows.imp_rat: Interlock override activation beyond the approved activity extends the safeguard-defeated window; relevant to override control with moderate consequence tied to duration.
rows.sat_rat: Overrides are rarely time-limited automatically, so extended override windows recur. This need is explicitly about limiting override activation duration. Keeping overrides brief depends on operator discipline to restore normal state. No significant cost driver.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of executing a transfer against obsolete or conflicting procedural instructions
rows.imp_rat: Executing a transfer against obsolete/conflicting instructions risks wrong procedures being followed; relevant to reliable operation with moderate consequence depending on the discrepancy.
rows.sat_rat: Document control reduces obsolete-procedure use, but execution against superseded instructions still occurs where control is manual. Checking current revision before execution adds a small lookup step. Relies on personnel knowing to verify the controlled version. Document control systems have moderate cost; paper controls are cheap but error-prone.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among adjacent-zone classifications, room-pressure targets, and transfer requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among classifications, pressure targets, and transfer requirements are design-consistency defects; relevant to correct cascade specification with moderate consequence, usually caught pre-operation.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-requirement conflicts are frequently left unresolved into detailed design, a recurring integration failure. Resolving classification and target conflicts adds coordination time across disciplines. Reconciling conflicting classification, pressure and transfer requirements demands senior integration judgement. Unresolved conflicts surfacing late are expensive to correct.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that concurrent room activities destabilize the required pressure cascade during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent room activities destabilizing the cascade during transfer is an external disturbance to boundary maintenance; relevant but generally transient with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Concurrent activity effects are frequently underanticipated, a recurring source of cascade excursions. Concurrent-activity effects are considered during design but assessment can extend analysis. Anticipating how concurrent room activities disturb the cascade requires operational insight often missing at spec stage. Mitigating concurrent disturbances may require added control capacity cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of residual contamination on prepared material exteriors before transfer
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination on prepared material exteriors before transfer risks carry-in; relevant to material transfer preparation with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Manual wipe-down leaves inconsistent residual contamination since verification is rarely quantitative at the point of transfer. Thorough wipe-down takes measurable time per item. Effectiveness depends heavily on operator technique and coverage discipline. Consumes disinfectant and wipes but at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a chamber-to-room seal loses boundary integrity under expected pressure conditions
rows.imp_rat: A chamber-to-room seal losing integrity under expected pressure is a boundary-integrity failure under normal conditions; relevant to containment with high consequence when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Seal integrity under expected pressure is well-served by proven gasket designs, though degradation over time remains. Seal integrity verification fits within normal qualification schedules. Seal selection and testing under pressure use well-known methods requiring standard skill. Quality seals add modest cost and periodic replacement expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unintended air leakage through the chamber boundary during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Unintended air leakage through the boundary during transfer erodes separation; relevant to containment integrity but usually a gradual, partial degradation, moderating severity.
rows.sat_rat: Gasketed doors and validated seals control leakage well when maintained, but degradation between tests creates recurring exposure. Leakage is a continuous state not a timed event, so time is not the primary lens. Detecting slow boundary leakage requires periodic testing skill and is not evident during routine use. Seal maintenance and leak testing consume ongoing labor and materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required material and personnel transfer scenarios omitted from the control specification
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required transfer scenarios from the control spec leaves unhandled cases; relevant to control completeness with moderate consequence depending on which scenarios are missed.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer scenarios are frequently under-specified, with edge cases omitted, so this need is only moderately served. Completeness of scenario coverage is a specification-time concern, not operational timing. Identifying every required transfer scenario demands thorough operational-analysis expertise. Comprehensive scenario capture adds specification labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of boundary-door opening beyond the allowable interval
rows.imp_rat: Boundary-door opening beyond the allowable interval extends the exposure window; relevant to containment with severity scaling by excess duration, generally moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Auto-close mechanisms and open-time alarms give reasonably consistent control but manual overrides erode it. This need is explicitly about limiting door-open duration, a direct time metric. Keeping doors open only briefly depends on operator discipline and habit. No significant cost dimension beyond lost cascade energy during opening.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of chamber state leaving required limits before transfer initiation
rows.imp_rat: Chamber state leaving required limits before transfer initiation risks starting non-conforming; relevant readiness concern with moderate consequence if other gates catch it.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks that gate initiation on chamber state make drifting-out-of-limits before start fairly well controlled. Chamber state is continuously read so detection is timely. Pre-initiation state checks are mostly automated, low skill. Standard sensing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between required chamber pressure behavior and facility pressure-control capacity
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between required chamber behavior and facility control capacity is a design-feasibility gap; relevant to sustainable cascade but moderate, since it manifests as pressure instability over time.
rows.sat_rat: Coordination gaps between chamber and facility systems make this a recurring integration weak point. Matching chamber behavior to facility capacity is done during design coordination without major delay. Reconciling chamber demand with HVAC capacity needs cross-disciplinary expertise that is often siloed. Capacity mismatches drive expensive AHU or damper rework if missed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of chamber pressure deviation from transfer limits at the readiness decision
rows.imp_rat: Chamber pressure deviation from transfer limits at the readiness decision risks starting from a non-conforming state; relevant readiness gate with moderate consequence if caught by other checks.
rows.sat_rat: Live DP display makes deviation at the decision point generally visible, though drift and calibration issues create occasional error. Pressure readouts are immediate so the readiness check itself is not slow. Interpreting deviation against transfer limits is mostly displayed, requiring modest judgement. Instrumentation cost is standard and already sunk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration that a chamber door remains open during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Duration a door remains open sets the exposure window for the boundary; relevant to containment with severity scaling by open time, generally moderate for a single door.
rows.sat_rat: Auto-closing doors and alarms help, but prolonged open states still occur during awkward loads. Door-open duration is the core time metric and is largely governed by how quickly the operator moves items through. Efficient loading to minimize open time depends on operator practice and staging discipline. Open-time overruns waste conditioned air but the incremental cost is minor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of material or personnel obstructing complete airlock-door closure during transfers
rows.imp_rat: Material/personnel obstructing complete door closure prevents the barrier from sealing; relevant to containment integrity with high consequence if a door cannot close.
rows.sat_rat: Position sensing and interlocks reliably flag incomplete closure in most modern airlocks, with occasional sensor faults as residual risk. Door sensors detect obstruction immediately, preventing a false closed state without delay. Sensors reduce dependence on operators noticing obstructions. Door position sensors are standard, low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of contaminant leakage around a closed chamber door during passage
rows.imp_rat: Contaminant leakage around a closed door during passage is a residual boundary-integrity issue; relevant but typically a lower-magnitude leak, giving moderate proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Compression seals perform well when new but degrade unpredictably, so around-door leakage recurs between inspections. Leakage around a closed door is a steady-state condition, not time-driven. Detecting seal leakage requires deliberate testing skill not present in routine operation. Seal replacement and testing are recurring maintenance costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the required pressure differential exceeds the pressure-control capacity of adjacent zones
rows.imp_rat: A required differential exceeding adjacent zones' control capacity risks unmet cascade; relevant to design feasibility with moderate consequence, typically caught in commissioning.
rows.sat_rat: Capacity-exceedance is sometimes discovered only during balancing, indicating an inconsistent practice. Checking differential against zone capacity is a quick engineering review. Assessing control capacity limits requires HVAC modeling skill not always applied at chamber spec stage. Over-specifying differentials wastes energy and control effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of boundary-door sealing failures after passage
rows.imp_rat: Boundary-door sealing failures after passage degrade the standing barrier; relevant to containment integrity with moderate consequence depending on leak severity.
rows.sat_rat: Door position sensors help but do not confirm seal integrity, so post-passage sealing failures still occur intermittently. Sealing failure is an event, not a duration concern. Confirming a proper seal often relies on operator observation rather than positive verification. Seal upkeep is a standard maintenance expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between validation acceptance criteria and chamber contamination-control requirements
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between validation acceptance criteria and contamination-control requirements is an assurance-design gap; relevant but indirect, allowing under-qualification with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: URS-to-protocol traceability practices reduce gross mismatches, but subtle divergences between stated criteria and real contamination-control intent remain common. Reconciling acceptance criteria against contamination-control requirements is an upfront protocol activity, not a schedule bottleneck. Aligning validation criteria to actual contamination-control needs relies heavily on experienced QA and validation authors, and mismatches persist where that expertise is thin. Misalignment surfaces late as rework and requalification, an avoidable resource drain rather than a direct outcome measure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of air leakage across the chamber boundary under specified pressure differentials
rows.imp_rat: Air leakage across the boundary under specified differentials degrades separation performance; relevant to containment integrity with moderate consequence, typically partial.
rows.sat_rat: Chamber leakage under differential is well-controlled by mature sealing and pressure-decay testing practice. Leakage testing is a routine qualification activity that fits standard timelines. Leak-tightness specification and testing methods are well-established and require only standard competence. Tighter seals add modest material cost, well understood in budgets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of chamber boundary surfaces left inadequately decontaminated before integrity verification
rows.imp_rat: Inadequately decontaminated boundary surfaces before integrity verification undermine the cleanliness assumption; relevant to preparation with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Vaporized agent distribution studies and indicator placement give reasonable assurance, but shadowed surfaces and hard-to-reach geometry still leave inconsistent coverage.
