Full-surface decontamination cycles are time-bounded and scheduled, so timing rarely drives this failure. Achieving uniform coverage of complex chamber geometries depends on operator technique and cycle-development expertise that is not evenly distributed. Incomplete decontamination triggers repeat cycles and consumable waste, a moderate cost consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between boundary-breach onset and operator recognition
rows.imp_rat: Duration until operator recognizes a breach affects how long contamination propagates, quite central to containment (relevance high); undetected breach is serious, held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without dedicated breach sensing, recognition delay is long and inconsistent. This need is the detection latency between breach onset and recognition. Recognition depends on operator attentiveness absent automated breach alarms. Detection instrumentation cost is often deferred.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material containers with sterile-barrier damage from chamber passage
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-barrier damage from chamber passage compromises product but is a material-handling concern; peripheral to boundary control with moderate-high consequence for the item.
rows.sat_rat: Barrier damage during passage is uncommon but occurs and often goes unverified until later. Not time-dominated. Avoiding sterile-barrier damage during passage requires careful handling technique. Damaged sterile barriers mean scrapped product, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of each item's required external surface area left untreated before transfer
rows.imp_rat: Untreated external surface area before transfer directly carries contamination across the boundary (relevance moderate); residual contamination on surfaces is a real ingress path, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Untreated surface fractions are common because manual coverage is inconsistent and unverified. Covering full surface area thoroughly adds wipe time. Complete coverage of complex geometries is highly technique-dependent. Minor consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of items arriving with surface contamination above the qualified decontamination range
rows.imp_rat: Items arriving with contamination above the qualified decon range means decon cannot reliably clean them (relevance moderate); over-burdened items carry contamination in, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Incoming contamination beyond the qualified range is largely outside the executor's control and detected inconsistently. Handling over-contaminated arrivals adds unplanned rework time. Judging contamination level on arrival requires experience. Rework and rejected items waste resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of airflow obstruction caused by material staged within the airlock or pass-through chamber
rows.imp_rat: Airflow obstruction from staged material affects local flow patterns; peripheral to the core boundary function with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Overloaded or poorly staged chambers routinely obstruct airflow without detection, a common uncontrolled condition. Airflow obstruction from staged material persists until noticed, with no immediate time signal. Recognizing airflow blockage requires understanding of chamber airflow patterns operators may lack. Shelving and staging aids are cheap but not always provided.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of additional transfers proceeding while a prior critical deviation remains unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Additional transfers proceeding with an unresolved critical deviation compounds a known risk (relevance moderate); proceeding under open deviation can propagate contamination, moderate-high severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation-management systems exist but pressure to keep operating means additional transfers often proceed before resolution. Holding further transfers pending deviation resolution can introduce operational delay. Requires QA judgement to decide when continued transfers are permissible. Holds and quarantines carry operational cost pressure that tempts continuation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of material leaking or spilling inside the chamber
rows.imp_rat: Material leaking or spilling inside the chamber contaminates it and possibly onward transfers; somewhat relevant to transfer control with moderate consequence, containable within the chamber.
rows.sat_rat: Spills inside the chamber are infrequent with good handling but not systematically prevented. Not a timing concern. Preventing spills depends on careful handling and correct securing by personnel. Cleanup and product loss are cost consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from breach recognition until isolation of the affected chamber
rows.imp_rat: Time from breach recognition to chamber isolation directly limits contamination spread, central to the job (relevance high); slow isolation worsens an active breach, but batch ceiling caps the pair.
rows.sat_rat: Manual isolation without automated triggers makes response time variable. This is explicitly about minimizing time from recognition to isolation. Fast isolation depends on operator recognizing and acting decisively. No major cost dimension.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of item surfaces receiving a decontaminant contact period outside the qualified range
rows.imp_rat: Decontaminant contact period outside the qualified range means decon may be ineffective, relevant to contamination control (relevance moderate); ineffective decon lets contamination through, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Contact time is frequently uncontrolled or estimated, so out-of-range dwell is a routine deviation. Maintaining qualified contact time forces timed dwell periods that operators may shortcut. Requires operator discipline to observe and time contact periods. No material cost beyond disinfectant.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of contact between cleaned items and contaminated chamber surfaces or unprepared items
rows.imp_rat: Cleaned items contacting contaminated surfaces or unprepared items recontaminates them (relevance moderate); recontamination defeats decon and enters the clean zone, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-contact between clean and dirty items in a shared chamber is a frequent, hard-to-prevent lapse. Keeping cleaned items separate adds handling steps. Depends on operator spatial discipline to avoid cross-contact. Recontamination causes rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transfer events lacking evidence that the pressure cascade and contamination boundary were preserved
rows.imp_rat: Lacking evidence of preserved cascade is an assurance/documentation gap; it does not itself breach the boundary but weakens verification, so both proxies are moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Many airlocks lack automated per-event pressure logging, so evidence gaps are common and the overseer routinely eats undocumented transfers. Capturing pressure-preservation evidence per transfer often adds manual logging time that overseers struggle to enforce consistently. Judging and recording that the cascade was preserved depends on trained personnel correctly interpreting and documenting conditions. Continuous evidence capture requires monitoring instrumentation or diligent paper records, a recurring resource burden.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transfer points with different contamination risks are assigned identical control logic
rows.imp_rat: Assigning identical control logic to transfer points of differing risk directly undermines boundary control at higher-risk points (relevance moderate); consequence is a mismatched protection level that could allow contamination, moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Identical logic is often applied across differing risk points as a shortcut, undermining protection and leaving this poorly served. Differentiating logic by risk is a design choice with no runtime timing effect. Tailoring logic to each transfer point's contamination risk requires nuanced risk-assessment expertise. Custom logic per point adds engineering cost versus copy-paste reuse.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect or unapproved material enters the chamber
rows.imp_rat: An incorrect/unapproved material entering the chamber is a material-control issue; somewhat peripheral to pressure/contamination boundary maintenance with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Manual approval verification catches most cases but unapproved items still slip through without automated gating. Verification of material approval adds a check step before entry. Correctly identifying approved vs unapproved material relies on operator knowledge and paperwork checks. Errors can waste rework but the check itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incomplete decontaminant contact before transfer
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete decontaminant contact before transfer means residual contamination crosses the boundary (relevance moderate); incomplete decon is a direct ingress path, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual application leaves incomplete-contact gaps that are hard to verify. Contact time is prescribed so speed is secondary. Ensuring complete decontaminant contact depends on operator coverage technique. Decontaminant is a recurring consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of decontamination residue remaining on released item surfaces before cleaner-zone entry
rows.imp_rat: Decontamination residue on released items is a product-quality/carryover concern; peripheral to the pressure/contamination boundary CFJ with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Visual inspection catches gross residue but sub-visible decon residue routinely goes unmeasured before entry. Allowing residue to clear adds drying/wiping time. Judging when residue is acceptably removed is operator-dependent. Residue waste and rework carry minor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a transfer violating qualified contamination-control procedures is verified as compliant
rows.imp_rat: A non-compliant transfer being verified as compliant defeats the control check and lets bad transfers pass (relevance moderate); false compliance can release contamination, moderately severe, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Non-compliant transfers are sometimes verified as compliant due to inconsistent, judgement-based review. Verification review adds time after the transfer. Correctly judging procedural compliance demands experienced reviewers who understand the qualified procedure. QA review labor is the main cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credible worst-case chamber configurations, loads, and transfer sequences omitted from the validation protocol
rows.imp_rat: Omitting worst-case configs/loads/sequences from validation leaves real transfer conditions unproven (relevance moderate); gaps can hide breach modes, moderate-high severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Protocols routinely omit credible edge configurations because coverage relies on manual brainstorming with no exhaustive method. Enumerating worst-case configurations is upfront work not driven by deadlines. Comprehensively capturing load and sequence permutations demands broad process expertise and is often incompletely done. Omitted configurations surface later as requalification cost, a downstream drain rather than the core loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between assumed chamber leakage and the leakage allowance in the room pressure balance
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between assumed chamber leakage and the room balance allowance is a design-margin error; relevant to cascade sustainability but indirect and typically caught in balancing, giving moderate-low proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Leakage-allowance mismatches between chamber vendor and HVAC designer are a common and often unnoticed gap. Reconciling leakage assumptions with the room balance is a quick coordination check. Aligning chamber leakage figures with room-balance allowances needs cross-team data exchange expertise. Discrepancies cause balancing rework cost if uncaught.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in required boundary states after loss of power or control communication
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in required boundary states after power/comms loss is central because fail-safe behavior protects the boundary during faults (relevance high); undefined fail states can leave zones open, serious but capped.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguity in post-loss states is common in specifications, so this need is only moderately served. Clarifying required states is documentation work, not operational timing. Specifying unambiguous post-loss boundary states requires precise technical-writing and analysis skill. Added specification effort, minimal hardware cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that staged materials obstruct chamber closure or required airflow
rows.imp_rat: Staged materials obstructing closure or airflow directly compromises the boundary seal and cascade (relevance high); obstruction can prevent closure and breach containment, moderately severe, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Obstruction of airflow or closure by staged loads is a recurring, often-unnoticed problem without spacing guides. Arranging staging to preserve airflow takes setup time. Requires operator understanding of airflow and closure clearances. No direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the qualification challenge and the expected contamination burden in sterile fill-finish transfers
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between challenge and expected burden in sterile fill-finish is a qualification-representativeness error in a high-stakes context (relevance moderate); understated challenge can leave sterile transfers unprotected, moderate-high severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile fill-finish guidance gives some structure, yet aligning the challenge to real transfer burden stays inconsistent across programs. Matching challenge to expected bioburden is set during protocol design, not a timing issue. Estimating the contamination burden of sterile fill-finish transfers requires specialized aseptic-process expertise that is scarce. An unrepresentative challenge invites failed media fills and investigation cost, but the root gap is analytical judgement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of quarantined or rejected material being accepted for transfer
rows.imp_rat: Accepting quarantined/rejected material for transfer is partly a material-status control error (relevance moderate); it can introduce contaminated material, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Status labeling and checks reduce but do not eliminate accepting quarantined material. Not primarily timing. Preventing acceptance of rejected material relies on status verification discipline. Errors cause rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting an item with a compromised container or outer wrap
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an item with a compromised container is a direct contamination-ingress path into the chamber (relevance moderate); breached packaging can release contaminants, moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Manual acceptance inspection catches most but not all compromised wraps, a recurring escape. Not time-driven. Catching compromised containers depends on inspector attention and training. Rejected items carry cost consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of inbound and outbound personnel encountering each other inside the chamber
rows.imp_rat: Inbound/outbound personnel meeting inside the chamber is largely a traffic/workflow concern; peripheral to pressure-cascade and contamination-boundary control with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Most designs are unidirectional or procedurally scheduled, but bidirectional conflicts inside the chamber still occur without hard controls. Coordinating directional flow can add waiting when both directions are busy. Avoiding encounters depends on staff communication and scheduling discipline. No notable cost element.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel entry overlaps an incompatible material transfer
rows.imp_rat: Personnel entry overlapping an incompatible material transfer is a scheduling/compatibility issue; peripheral to core boundary control with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Separate personnel and material airlocks in good facilities help, but shared chambers leave real overlap risk. Segregating personnel and material transfers can require sequencing delays. Depends on operator awareness of compatibility rules, which is judgement-heavy. No significant cost dimension.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of chamber occupancy exceeding the permitted limit during entry
rows.imp_rat: Chamber occupancy exceeding the permitted limit is more an operational/procedural constraint; peripheral to pressure/contamination boundary control with moderate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Occupancy control is typically procedural rather than sensor-enforced, so overcrowding during busy periods recurs. Occupancy limits rarely add delay unless the chamber is congested. Enforcement relies on personnel self-monitoring and supervision rather than automated counting. No direct material cost implication.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that damaged packaging releases contaminants inside the chamber
rows.imp_rat: Damaged packaging releasing contaminants inside the chamber is a direct breach of the contamination boundary (relevance moderate); active release inside is serious, capped by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visual inspection catches obvious damage, yet subtle breaches releasing contaminants inside still occur. Inspecting packaging integrity adds a brief check. Spotting damaged packaging depends on operator attentiveness. Damaged-package spills can waste product but inspection is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that staged materials prevent complete chamber-door closure
rows.imp_rat: Staged materials preventing complete door closure directly defeats the physical seal (relevance high); an unclosed door breaches the boundary, moderately severe, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Door sensors flag failure to close, so obstruction of closure is usually caught quickly. Checking clearance before closing adds seconds. Depends on operator visual judgement of fit. No significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a qualified transfer practice is changed without reassessing its sterility-preserving capability
rows.imp_rat: Changing a qualified transfer practice without reassessing sterility capability is a change-control error (relevance moderate); unassessed changes can silently break sterility, moderate-high severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Formal change control is mature and widely enforced, so unassessed changes are relatively uncommon. Change control review adds lead time before practice changes. Reassessing sterility impact of a change requires qualified change-control expertise. Change control administration consumes QA resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of environmental differential excursions below the required classification limit at transfer points
rows.imp_rat: Environmental differential excursions below classification limits at transfer points directly indicate a compromised cascade (relevance high); excursions can permit ingress, moderately severe, held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Differential excursions at transfer points recur during active use and are not always continuously monitored, leaving gaps. Excursions below classification limits are flagged quickly by monitoring where installed. Interpreting environmental excursions requires trained review. Particle and differential monitoring adds equipment cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of personnel transfers involving individuals without current qualification
rows.imp_rat: Actual unqualified personnel transferring is more directly a contamination risk than the record gap (relevance moderate); unqualified handling can breach the boundary, moderate severity, capped by batch.
rows.sat_rat: LMS tracking catches most lapsed qualifications, but expired individuals occasionally still perform transfers before flags are acted on. Qualification status is generally known in advance, so no transfer delay. Relies on training coordinators keeping qualifications current. Training management systems are a standard operating cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that displayed pressure readings misrepresent actual zone differentials during transfers
rows.imp_rat: Displayed pressure readings misrepresenting actual differentials hides real cascade state (relevance high); false readings can mask an active breach, moderate-high severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated instruments generally represent true differentials well, though drift between calibrations can occasionally mislead. Gauge calibration checks are periodic, not per-transfer delays. Interpreting readings is simple; calibration relies on metrology technicians. Calibration programs are a standard recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the allowable pressure differential range between adjacent classified zones
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in the allowable pressure differential range is fairly central because pressure cascade is core to the job (relevance moderate); an undefined range can permit a breach, but it is a design-specification gap rather than an active failure, so severity is moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Differential ranges are often stated only as minimums, leaving allowable-range ambiguity that recurs. Defining an allowable differential range is a fast specification step. Setting a defensible range needs understanding of both control capacity and containment, moderate skill. Ambiguous ranges cause rework but the specification itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a transfer load prevents complete closure of a zone-facing boundary
rows.imp_rat: A transfer load preventing complete closure of a zone-facing boundary directly defeats the seal (relevance high); an unclosed boundary breaches containment, moderate-high severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Load-obstructed closure is largely prevented by clearance design and door-sensor interlocks, uncommon in practice. Load-clearance design is set at specification and does not delay transfers. Sizing chamber and openings for loads uses standard mechanical design skill. Adequate clearances add little cost when designed early.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between acceptance thresholds and sterile fill-finish contamination-control requirements
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between acceptance thresholds and sterile fill-finish requirements risks accepting unsafe conditions in a high-stakes context (relevance moderate); misaligned thresholds can pass contamination into sterile product, moderate-high severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Guidance drives partial alignment but subtle threshold mismatches persist for sterile contexts. Threshold alignment is set during planning, not a timing concern. Aligning thresholds to sterile fill-finish requirements demands specialized regulatory and aseptic knowledge. Misaligned thresholds cause costly late corrections, secondary to the alignment failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a contamination-boundary breach during material or personnel transfer between classified zones
rows.imp_rat: A contamination-boundary breach during transfer is essentially the failure of the core job (relevance high); a breach is the worst direct consequence, but the batch ceiling caps the mean at 5.
rows.sat_rat: Mature interlocked airlock designs make contamination-boundary breaches during transfer relatively uncommon, though procedural lapses still occur. Interlock and cascade controls operate in real time without adding transfer delay once installed. Modern airlock designs embed the control logic so operators need limited skill to maintain the boundary. Proven interlocked airlock technology is available at established cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of pressure-cascade excursions during material or personnel transfer
rows.imp_rat: Pressure-cascade excursions during transfer directly undermine the cascade the job must preserve (relevance high); excursions can drive reverse flow, moderately severe, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Differential-pressure interlocks and alarms make cascade excursions during transfer infrequent under established design practice. Pressure-cascade monitoring is continuous and does not slow transfers. Standard BMS and differential-pressure controls reduce reliance on operator vigilance. Cascade control instrumentation is a well-understood cost line.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of contamination on protective-attire exterior surfaces at classified-zone entry
rows.imp_rat: Contamination on protective-attire exteriors at zone entry carries contaminants into the clean zone (relevance moderate); attire-borne contamination is a real ingress path, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Attire-surface contamination is largely invisible and rarely measured at entry, leaving it poorly controlled. Inspecting attire exterior contamination is quick. Detecting contamination on attire is difficult by eye and skill-dependent. No significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credible transfer and facility states omitted from the cross-contamination airflow evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Transfer/facility states omitted from cross-contamination airflow evaluation leaves airflow paths unassessed (relevance moderate); omissions can permit cross-contamination, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-contamination airflow evaluations routinely omit some credible states, a persistent coverage weakness. Enumerating credible states extends the analysis effort. Covering all credible transfer and facility states demands strong hazard-analysis expertise. Omitted states cause expensive late findings during qualification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfer-associated excursions unresolved when sterility assurance is judged
rows.imp_rat: Transfer-associated excursions unresolved at sterility-assurance judgment is a disposition-timeliness error (relevance moderate); unresolved excursions at release risk shipping compromised product, moderate-high severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Excursions frequently linger unresolved when sterility is judged, a recurring gap. Unresolved excursions at the sterility-assurance judgement point reflect timing shortfalls. Closing excursions demands skilled root-cause work. Prolonged open items tie up quality resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of intended transfer conditions unsupported by validation evidence
rows.imp_rat: Intended transfer conditions unsupported by validation evidence leaves operating conditions unproven (relevance moderate); unvalidated conditions may fail in use, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Some intended transfer conditions run without supporting validation evidence, a recurring gap between practice and qualification. Validation studies are lengthy up-front activities. Establishing validation evidence for all conditions requires specialized validation expertise. Broad validation coverage is a substantial cost that limits scope.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between verification conditions and defined transfer, occupancy, and zone-pressure conditions
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between verification conditions and actual transfer/occupancy/pressure conditions weakens verification validity (relevance moderate); it can validate the wrong conditions, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Verification conditions commonly mismatch actual operating conditions, a recurring validation gap. Aligning verification to defined conditions is planning effort, not runtime. Matching verification conditions to real transfer/occupancy/pressure states requires strong V&V expertise. Comprehensive verification design adds cost often trimmed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residue from a prior transfer contacts prepared materials
rows.imp_rat: Residue from a prior transfer contacting prepared materials is carryover contamination (relevance moderate); carryover contaminates clean product, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Residue carryover between transfers is common where chamber cleaning is not verified each cycle. Cleaning the chamber between transfers adds turnaround time. Depends on operator diligence in inter-transfer cleaning. Cleaning consumables and time cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cleaned surfaces being recontaminated before the next transfer
rows.imp_rat: Cleaned surfaces recontaminated before next transfer negates prior decontamination (relevance moderate); recontamination reintroduces hazard, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaned surfaces are frequently recontaminated before next transfer without protective controls. The window between cleaning and use is a factor but not the core loss. Preventing recontamination depends on careful handling and staging discipline. Rework of recontaminated surfaces adds cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sanitized item exteriors being recontaminated before zone entry
rows.imp_rat: Sanitized item exteriors recontaminated before zone entry negates the sanitization (relevance moderate); recontamination introduces contaminants at entry, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitized exteriors are readily recontaminated during staging, a recurring gap. Not primarily timing. Preventing exterior recontamination before entry depends on handling discipline. Rework adds cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exterior contamination spreading to chamber surfaces or adjacent items during sanitization
rows.imp_rat: Exterior contamination spreading during sanitization defeats the sanitization step (relevance moderate); spread contaminates chamber and adjacent items, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual sanitization frequently spreads contamination to adjacent surfaces without segregation controls. Not time-dominated. Avoiding cross-contamination during sanitization depends on ordered technique and separation. Additional supplies and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of stalled materials losing required sterility or physical condition
rows.imp_rat: Stalled materials losing sterility or physical condition is a hold/queue quality error (relevance moderate); loss of sterility ruins product, moderate-high severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Stalled-material condition loss is handled reactively and inconsistently. Duration of stall matters but the loss is compromised sterility/condition. Judging whether stalled material is still acceptable requires expertise. Loss of stalled material is direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual chamber contamination at the readiness decision
rows.imp_rat: Residual chamber contamination at the readiness decision means the chamber is not clean when declared ready (relevance moderate); residual contamination contaminates next transfer, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Residual contamination at the readiness decision is usually assumed clean rather than confirmed, leaving uncertainty. Not time-driven. Judging residual contamination at readiness depends on inference rather than measurement skill. Sampling adds cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that selected challenge locations exclude the chamber areas hardest to decontaminate
rows.imp_rat: Challenge locations excluding hardest-to-decontaminate areas is a validation-representativeness error (relevance moderate); it can leave worst spots unproven, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Indicator-placement guidance exists but consistently identifying the truly hardest spots remains judgement-dependent and error-prone. Selecting challenge locations is a planning task not gated by schedule. Knowing which chamber areas are hardest to decontaminate demands specialized coverage and airflow knowledge that many teams lack. Missing worst-case locations means costly requalification, though the primary gap is knowledge not resource.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in acceptance conditions distinguishing safe and unsafe state transitions
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous acceptance conditions distinguishing safe/unsafe transitions is a criteria-clarity error central to interlock safety (relevance moderate); ambiguity can permit unsafe transitions, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguous acceptance conditions are common, so evaluators disagree and the need is only moderately served. Clarifying acceptance conditions is a documentation activity with no runtime impact. Writing unambiguous safe/unsafe transition criteria requires disciplined specification skill. Modest added documentation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether every pressure-boundary interface is covered by the evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about whether every pressure interface is covered by the criteria is a coverage-assurance gap (relevance moderate); uncovered interfaces can breach unnoticed, moderate severity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of decontaminant concentration deviation from the approved range
rows.imp_rat: Decontaminant concentration deviation from approved range reduces decon efficacy (relevance moderate); under-strength decon leaves contamination, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual mixing produces recurring concentration deviations without inline verification. Not time-dominated. Maintaining concentration in range depends on correct preparation and measurement skill. Concentration control affects consumable use cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of readiness declarations made while acceptance conditions remain unmet
rows.imp_rat: Readiness declarations made while acceptance conditions are unmet is a false-ready control error (relevance moderate); declaring ready prematurely lets unsafe transfers proceed, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Readiness declared with unmet conditions still happens without enforced checklists. Not time-dominated. Confirming all acceptance conditions before declaring ready depends on operator thoroughness. Premature declarations cause rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residues from an earlier transfer contaminating current materials
rows.imp_rat: Residues from an earlier transfer contaminating current materials is cross-contamination carryover (relevance moderate); it introduces prior contaminants into product, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Residual carryover between transfers persists without verified cleaning cycles. Not time-driven. Avoiding residue carryover depends on thorough between-transfer cleaning technique. Extra cleaning consumes consumables and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved chamber alarms or faults at the readiness decision
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved alarms/faults at the readiness decision means declaring ready with open issues (relevance moderate); proceeding with faults risks breach, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved alarms at readiness are a recurring source of masked risk. Clearing alarms takes time but the loss is unresolved state. Distinguishing real from nuisance faults requires skill. No major cost driver.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel are admitted in an order inconsistent with cleanliness status or assigned activity
rows.imp_rat: Personnel admitted in an order inconsistent with cleanliness status can cross-contaminate cleaner zones (relevance moderate); wrong sequencing spreads contamination, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Order-of-entry control is procedural and prone to lapses under busy conditions. Sequencing by cleanliness status can add coordination delay. Requires operator judgement about proper admission order. No direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of personnel admitted without current authorization for the destination classified zone
rows.imp_rat: Personnel admitted without current authorization for the destination zone is an access-control error (relevance moderate); unauthorized entry can breach the clean zone, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Badge/access systems make unauthorized entry uncommon where electronic access control gates zones. Authorization checks add brief delay at entry. Verifying authorization is routine but relies on operator or badge system. Little cost implication.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of applying a decontamination requirement incompatible with the item or destination zone
rows.imp_rat: Applying a decontamination requirement incompatible with item or zone can damage items or fail decon (relevance moderate); wrong decon leaves contamination or harms product, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Procedural matrices help, but incompatible-requirement errors persist where guidance is ambiguous. Selecting the right decon requirement adds minimal time if procedures are clear. Matching decontaminant to item and destination zone demands specific knowledge and is error-prone. Wrong agent can damage items or waste materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tears or punctures in protective attire at classified-zone entry
rows.imp_rat: Tears or punctures in protective attire at entry compromise the personnel barrier (relevance moderate); breached attire introduces contamination, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Small tears often go undetected since inspection is visual and cursory. Inspecting attire for tears takes seconds. Spotting punctures depends on visual attentiveness. Replacing damaged attire is a minor consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of skin exposed by poorly fitted protective attire at classified-zone entry
rows.imp_rat: Skin exposed by poorly fitted attire at entry is a direct personnel contamination source (relevance moderate); exposed skin sheds contaminants into the zone, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mirror and buddy checks catch gross exposure but subtle skin exposure gaps recur. A fit check adds only moments. Recognizing poor fit relies on observation and self-check. No direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of personnel reaching classified-zone entry without current gowning qualification or required health clearance
rows.imp_rat: Personnel reaching entry without gowning qualification or health clearance is a personnel-readiness control error (relevance moderate); unqualified/ill personnel can contaminate the zone, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification tracking is often manual, so lapsed clearances sometimes slip through at entry. Verifying gowning qualification and health clearance adds a check step. Depends on records lookup and supervisor judgement. Minimal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an affected transfer proceeds before the preparation deficiency is corrected
rows.imp_rat: An affected transfer proceeding before a preparation deficiency is corrected proceeds under a known gap (relevance moderate); proceeding uncorrected risks contamination, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Without hard holds, transfers sometimes proceed before deficiencies are corrected. Holding an affected transfer for correction adds delay. Depends on operator recognizing the deficiency and enforcing hold. Rework cost if proceeding erroneously.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of prepared materials contacting non-designated chamber surfaces during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Prepared materials contacting non-designated surfaces during transfer recontaminates them (relevance moderate); contact spreads contamination to product, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Contact with non-designated surfaces occurs regularly without dedicated fixturing. Careful placement adds marginal time. Avoiding non-designated surface contact relies on operator care. Minor rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of prepared materials that violate destination-zone entry restrictions
rows.imp_rat: Prepared materials violating destination-zone entry restrictions is a material-eligibility control error (relevance moderate); ineligible material entering a clean zone contaminates it, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual checks catch many violations but zone-restriction breaches recur without systematic gating. Checking destination restrictions adds a verification step. Requires knowledge of zone-specific entry rules. Violations cause rework and possible zone remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfer evaluations applying incorrect or unapproved contamination-boundary acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Applying incorrect boundary acceptance criteria in transfer evaluations can pass unsafe transfers (relevance moderate); wrong criteria falsely accept breaches, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Incorrect or outdated acceptance criteria are applied occasionally where document control is weak. Applying criteria correctly adds little time when criteria are clear. Using correct, approved acceptance criteria depends on reviewer training and access to current standards. Minimal direct cost beyond review effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which qualification conditions differ from routine and worst-case transfer conditions
rows.imp_rat: Qualification conditions differing from routine/worst-case transfer conditions undermines validity of qualification (relevance moderate); unrepresentative qualification masks real failures, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification conditions often diverge from real routine and worst-case transfers, weakening assurance. Matching qualification to worst-case conditions extends study duration. Designing representative worst-case qualification demands specialized expertise. Worst-case testing increases qualification cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credible material and personnel transfer scenarios omitted from qualification scope
rows.imp_rat: Transfer scenarios omitted from qualification scope leaves credible cases unproven (relevance moderate); unqualified scenarios can fail in use, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Credible worst-case scenarios are frequently omitted from qualification because scoping depends on fallible expert foresight. Qualification scoping is a front-loaded activity not under routine time pressure. Identifying all credible transfer scenarios requires deep process expertise and risk-analysis skill. Broader qualification scope raises study cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about boundary integrity while material or personnel crosses between classified zones
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about boundary integrity during a live crossing is central to knowing the job succeeded (relevance high); prolonged doubt undermines assurance rather than being a breach itself, moderate severity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of pressure alarms configured inconsistently with validated excursion limits
rows.imp_rat: Pressure alarms configured inconsistently with validated limits can fail to warn of excursions (relevance moderate); mis-set alarms delay breach detection, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm setpoints are usually aligned to validated limits, but configuration drift and change-management lapses create occasional mismatches. Alarm configuration is a one-time setup activity. Setting alarms to validated limits requires qualified configuration personnel. Configuration cost is minor within BMS/monitoring systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of a pressure-boundary excursion after all zone-facing boundaries are closed
rows.imp_rat: A pressure-boundary excursion persisting after boundaries are closed indicates recovery failure (relevance moderate); prolonged excursion prolongs breach risk, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery is generally achieved, but its duration varies with control responsiveness. Excursion recovery duration after closure is a real transient that monitoring flags but recovery still takes measurable time. Minimizing recovery time needs control-tuning expertise that is unevenly applied. Faster recovery requires higher-capacity control adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of relevant contamination hazards omitted from decontamination acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Relevant hazards omitted from decontamination acceptance criteria means decon may not target real contaminants (relevance moderate); missed hazards allow ingress, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Hazard analyses help but relevant hazards are still routinely missed from acceptance criteria, leaving gaps. Hazard identification for acceptance criteria is done during protocol development without acute timing pressure. Enumerating relevant contamination hazards depends on cross-disciplinary expertise that is unevenly applied. Omitted hazards create investigation and rework costs later, secondary to the coverage failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of internal chamber regions excluded from decontamination acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Internal chamber regions excluded from decon acceptance criteria leaves reservoirs uncontrolled (relevance moderate); untreated regions harbor contamination, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria commonly omit awkward internal regions because coverage decisions are judgement-based and inconsistent. Defining which internal regions the criteria cover is upfront and not schedule-bound. Knowing which internal regions matter requires geometry and airflow expertise that is specialized. Excluded regions drive later rework, a secondary cost consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that challenge conditions do not represent worst-case intended chamber use
rows.imp_rat: Challenge conditions not representing worst-case use is a qualification-adequacy error that can leave real breaches unvalidated (relevance moderate); it undermines assurance rather than causing an immediate breach, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Risk-assessment frameworks help, but whether the challenge truly represents worst case hinges on expert judgement and is frequently incomplete. Defining worst-case challenge conditions is front-loaded design work with no acute time pressure. Correctly identifying worst-case use requires deep process knowledge and imagination about failure modes, and inexperienced teams routinely under-challenge. Under-representative challenges lead to expensive late failures, but the direct loss is missed expertise not spent money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible worst-case transfer states omitted from boundary qualification
rows.imp_rat: Worst-case transfer states omitted from boundary qualification leaves credible breach conditions unvalidated (relevance moderate); unproven states risk undetected breach, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage of transfer states is frequently partial because there is no systematic method guaranteeing completeness. Boundary-qualification state enumeration is planning work, not time-constrained. Identifying all worst-case transfer states needs experienced evaluators who understand dynamic boundary behavior. Missed states cause rework cost but the primary consequence is qualification incompleteness.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in allowable pressure differentials for defined transfer states
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in allowable differentials for defined transfer states is a specification-precision error on the cascade (relevance moderate); ambiguity can allow out-of-range operation, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-differential limits are often stated but with ambiguity across states, giving moderate service. Defining allowable differentials is design-time work, not operational timing. Setting unambiguous pressure-differential ranges per state needs process and containment expertise. Little added cost beyond engineering definition effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that the contamination boundary was preserved after transfer
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that the boundary was preserved after transfer is a post-hoc assurance gap (relevance moderate); prolonged uncertainty delays release decisions, moderate severity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of foreseeable abnormal transfer conditions lacking authorized handling criteria
rows.imp_rat: Foreseeable abnormal transfer conditions lacking authorized handling criteria leaves gaps for off-nominal events (relevance moderate); no handling rules risk improvised, unsafe responses, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures typically cover normal flows while many foreseeable abnormal conditions lack authorized handling, forcing improvisation. Defining abnormal-condition handling is an up-front development effort, not a runtime delay. Anticipating and codifying abnormal transfer scenarios demands scarce foresight and process expertise. Comprehensive scenario development is expensive and often deprioritized.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible chamber fault fault states lacking pressure-boundary acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Chamber fault states lacking pressure-boundary acceptance criteria leaves fault behavior undefined (relevance moderate); undefined fault states can breach during failures, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Fault-state pressure-boundary criteria are frequently missing, a notable reliability gap. Adding fault-state criteria extends the analysis effort somewhat. Anticipating credible chamber fault states demands failure-analysis expertise often underapplied. Unaddressed fault states cause expensive incident remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of interlock requirement conflicts across specifications, risk records, and design records
rows.imp_rat: Interlock requirement conflicts across documents is a design-consistency error affecting boundary protection logic (relevance moderate); conflicts can yield unsafe interlock behavior, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement conflicts across document sets are a chronic, poorly managed problem, so this is weakly served. Resolving cross-document conflicts is review effort not tied to runtime. Reconciling requirements across specs, risk and design records demands systems-engineering rigor that is scarce. Cross-record reconciliation is labor-intensive and often under-resourced.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in rationales linking interlock logic to pressure-cascade and contamination-boundary protection
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous rationales linking interlock logic to protection is a traceability/design-justification error (relevance moderate); weak rationale can allow unjustified logic, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Rationale-to-logic linkage is frequently thin or implicit, so this need is poorly served. Documenting rationales is upfront effort, not runtime delay. Articulating clear links between logic and containment intent requires deep domain reasoning that is scarce. Rationale documentation adds analytical labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cleaning or maintenance states lacking specified barrier and pressure-cascade behavior
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning/maintenance states lacking specified barrier/cascade behavior leaves the boundary undefined during those states (relevance moderate); undefined maintenance states can breach containment, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning and maintenance states are commonly omitted from interlock specs, a well-known recurring gap. Specifying cleaning/maintenance-state behavior is upfront work, not runtime delay. Covering maintenance and cleaning states needs operational insight often overlooked by designers. Added specification effort; little hardware cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which contamination-boundary and pressure-cascade consequences are understated in the investment case
rows.imp_rat: Understating contamination/pressure consequences in an investment case is an upstream funding-narrative error, peripheral to the physical control job (relevance low); its consequence is misfunded projects rather than an immediate breach, so severity is moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo cases routinely understate these failure consequences because there is no standard method to translate technical breach risk into financial terms, so the outcome varies widely by author. Building a defensible consequence narrative takes considerable elapsed time as Quality and Engineering inputs are gathered iteratively for the investment case. Accurately articulating contamination-boundary and pressure-cascade consequences demands scarce cross-disciplinary judgement that few purchase executors possess unaided. Understated consequences risk under-funding, but the direct cost of writing the case is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material and personnel transfers lacking contemporaneous evidence that contamination-boundary conditions remained acceptable
rows.imp_rat: Transfers lacking contemporaneous evidence that boundary conditions held is a records/assurance gap (relevance moderate); it undermines proof rather than the boundary itself, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporaneous boundary-condition evidence is frequently incomplete because recording is manual and secondary to the task. Generating contemporaneous evidence adds documentation time to each transfer. Capturing acceptable-condition evidence relies on operator diligence in recording. Manual logging is low cost but labor-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of undetected procedural deviations after transfers
rows.imp_rat: Undetected procedural deviations persisting after transfers means problems go uncaught (relevance moderate); delayed detection lets issues recur, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Without real-time deviation detection, lapses can remain unnoticed for extended periods, a chronic weakness. Undetected deviations by definition persist until a later review, so detection latency is long. Catching deviations after the fact relies on skilled reviewers spotting anomalies. Review effort is costly and typically periodic rather than continuous.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of decontaminant residue remaining on item exteriors at zone entry
rows.imp_rat: Decontaminant residue on item exteriors at zone entry introduces chemical residue into the clean zone (relevance moderate); residue is a contamination/quality issue, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Residue at zone entry is inconsistently controlled and rarely verified. Residue removal timing is minor. Removing decontaminant residue depends on rinse/wipe technique. Extra consumables for residue removal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity created by conflicting chamber boundary indications
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting chamber boundary indications create ambiguity about the true boundary state (relevance moderate); acting on a wrong indication can mask a breach, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting indications are common in status-quo panels and resolution is inconsistent. Resolving ambiguity takes time but the loss is the ambiguity itself. Reconciling conflicting boundary indications demands experienced interpretation. No major cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the transfer interface remains outside the ready-state pressure range after transfer
rows.imp_rat: Transfer interface remaining outside ready-state pressure range after transfer is a recovery/readiness timing concern (relevance moderate); prolonged off-range delays safe next use, low-moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: HVAC restores ready-state pressure fairly consistently though recovery duration varies. This is explicitly the time the interface stays outside ready-state pressure after transfer. Recovery is automatic, requiring little skill. Faster recovery needs more HVAC energy/capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of material, packaging, or debris left inside the chamber after transfer
rows.imp_rat: Material/debris left in the chamber after transfer is a housekeeping/waste error (relevance low-moderate); residual debris can harbor contamination but is manageable, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Post-transfer chamber clearance is mostly reliable with routine housekeeping but occasional debris remains. Not a speed issue. Clearing debris after transfer depends on operator diligence. Minimal cost dimension.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among door, pressure, and readiness indications after transfer
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among door, pressure, and readiness indications after transfer confuses the readiness call (relevance moderate); conflicting signals can lead to a wrong readiness decision, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Indication disagreement after transfer occurs and lacks automated arbitration. Not primarily timing. Reconciling door/pressure/readiness disagreements requires operator judgement. No significant cost dimension.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting material beyond its approved use or hold limit
rows.imp_rat: Accepting material beyond its use/hold limit is a material-status control error, moderately relevant to what crosses (relevance moderate); expired material is a quality issue more than a boundary breach, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual limit checks are error-prone and expired/over-limit acceptance still occurs. Not a speed issue. Checking hold/use limits depends on operator reading records correctly. Expired-material use causes waste cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required chamber-state evidence missing or stale at the readiness decision
rows.imp_rat: Missing or stale chamber-state evidence at the readiness decision weakens the basis for that decision (relevance moderate); deciding on stale data can miss an unsafe state, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or stale state evidence at readiness is common where data isn't time-stamped or enforced. Stale evidence has a time aspect but the loss is the missing certainty. Knowing which evidence is stale requires operator awareness. No major cost driver.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lingering doubt that a boundary breach escaped notice during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Lingering doubt that a breach escaped notice is an assurance/detection-confidence concern (relevance moderate); it affects trust in containment rather than being a breach, moderate severity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of staged materials whose identity or entry status cannot be verified
rows.imp_rat: Staged materials whose identity/entry status can't be verified is a traceability/eligibility error (relevance moderate); unverifiable materials risk improper entry, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Unverifiable items are caught inconsistently when labeling or paperwork is incomplete. Verifying entry status of staged items adds check time. Depends on operator reconciling labels with records. Minor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that prepared material identity, lot, or disposition status remains unconfirmed before transfer
rows.imp_rat: Unconfirmed material identity/lot/disposition before transfer is a material-verification error (relevance moderate); wrong or unapproved material entering is a quality/contamination risk, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual disposition confirmation leaves gaps where status is assumed rather than verified. Confirming identity/lot/disposition adds a records check. Requires operator to cross-reference documentation accurately. Minor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that prepared material is released before required drying conditions are met
rows.imp_rat: Prepared material released before required drying can carry residual liquid/decon (relevance moderate); premature release risks residue and efficacy issues, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Premature release before drying is a frequent deviation because drying is rarely timed or verified. Waiting for drying imposes real dwell time that gets shortcut under pressure. Judging adequate drying is subjective without indicators. No material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in transfer execution across personnel, shifts, and transfer directions
rows.imp_rat: Variation in transfer execution across personnel/shifts/directions creates inconsistent control (relevance moderate); variability raises breach likelihood but each instance is bounded, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Execution variation across operators and shifts is a persistent, poorly controlled source of inconsistency. Variation itself does not primarily cost time. Consistency across personnel, shifts and directions depends heavily on training and behavioral standardization. Training programs to reduce variation carry ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of classification recovery after a material or personnel transfer
rows.imp_rat: Classification recovery duration after transfer is a throughput/readiness concern, moderately relevant to maintaining the cascade (relevance moderate); a slow recovery delays next use but rarely causes irreversible harm, so severity low-moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery times are consistent under design conditions but degrade with filter loading or heavy transfers. Classification recovery duration is the core throughput constraint and is generally predictable. Recovery is largely automatic, requiring little operator skill. Faster recovery drives higher air-change energy costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transfer events lacking complete evidence of contamination-control procedure execution
rows.imp_rat: Transfers lacking complete evidence of procedure execution is a documentation-completeness gap (relevance moderate); it weakens assurance more than the boundary, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer records commonly lack complete execution evidence, a chronic documentation gap. Assembling complete execution evidence is time-consuming at review. Knowing what constitutes complete evidence requires procedural expertise. Documentation burden consumes labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt about which boundary-status indication to trust during a classified-zone crossing
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about which boundary-status indication to trust during a crossing hampers real-time decisions (relevance moderate); trusting a wrong indicator can mask a breach, moderate severity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts between procedural requirements and approved classification, flow, or validation requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts between procedures and approved classification/flow/validation requirements is a consistency error (relevance moderate); conflicts can drive wrong execution, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts between procedures and approved requirements are frequently latent and surface only during audits or incidents. Reconciling procedures against classification and validation baselines is a slow periodic review. Detecting conflicts demands cross-disciplinary expertise across QA, validation, and facility engineering. Comprehensive conflict review consumes significant specialist labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of authorizing a superseded or uncontrolled procedure revision for GMP use
rows.imp_rat: Authorizing a superseded procedure revision for GMP is a document-control error, largely compliance-oriented (relevance low-moderate); wrong revision can cause incorrect execution, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mature document-control practice makes authorizing superseded revisions uncommon, though it still happens with hybrid paper systems. Revision control approval is an up-front workflow, not a transfer-time delay. Document control roles handle revision authorization with defined procedures. EDMS or controlled-copy systems carry moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of in-scope material and personnel transfer scenarios omitted from the procedure
rows.imp_rat: In-scope transfer scenarios omitted from the procedure leaves operators without guidance (relevance moderate); missing procedures risk improvised unsafe transfers, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures commonly omit less-frequent transfer scenarios, leaving them uncontrolled until an event exposes the gap. Scope completeness is a design-phase concern, not operational timing. Ensuring all in-scope scenarios are captured requires experienced authors who know the full transfer landscape. Exhaustive scenario coverage adds authoring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material or personnel transfer events lacking a required record
rows.imp_rat: Transfer events lacking a required record is a records-completeness/traceability error (relevance moderate); mainly compliance and traceability exposure, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Required records are standard practice, but missing records for unlogged transfers remain a recurring finding. Creating a record per transfer adds routine logging time. Depends on operator discipline to log every event. Logbooks are cheap; enforcement is the real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfer events documented against an obsolete or unapproved procedure revision
rows.imp_rat: Transfers documented against obsolete revisions is a document-control/records error (relevance low-moderate); mainly compliance exposure, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled forms usually carry current revisions, but documentation against obsolete revisions recurs during transition periods. Referencing the approved revision on a record adds negligible time. Depends on personnel citing the correct controlled revision. Low cost where document control is in place.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfer deviations lacking documented investigation, disposition, or linkage to the affected event
rows.imp_rat: Deviations lacking documented investigation/disposition is a quality-records completeness error (relevance moderate); it weakens root-cause control, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Formal deviation systems capture most events, but linkage to the specific affected transfer is inconsistently maintained. Investigation and disposition workflows are lengthy but not real-time constrained. Proper deviation investigation and linkage requires trained QA investigators. Deviation management is labor-intensive and costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required for differential pressure to return to validated limits after door closure
rows.imp_rat: Time for differential pressure to return to limits after door closure is a recovery-speed concern (relevance moderate); slow recovery extends vulnerability but usually recovers, low-moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed airlocks recover to limits quickly and consistently, though undersized systems recover slowly. Recovery time after door closure is a designed HVAC characteristic, typically fast in balanced systems. No operator skill affects recovery time once designed. Fast recovery depends on air-handling capacity investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of relevant idle, loading, unloading, and boundary-opening states omitted from the pressure requirements
rows.imp_rat: Idle/loading/unloading/opening states omitted from pressure requirements leaves boundary states undefined (relevance moderate); undefined states can breach the cascade, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Idle and boundary-opening states are frequently omitted from pressure requirements, a recurring gap. Adding idle/loading/unloading states to requirements is quick once identified. Recognizing all relevant operating states requires disciplined state enumeration skill. Omitted states cause later specification amendments.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the reference locations at which the required pressure differential applies
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in reference locations where the differential applies is a specification-precision error affecting measurement of the cascade (relevance moderate); mislocated criteria could mask a breach but is a definition gap, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Reference-location ambiguity is a frequent, under-addressed gap in pressure specifications. Specifying reference measurement locations is quick once decided. Choosing correct reference points requires knowing measurement conventions many specs treat loosely. Ambiguous reference points cause requalification cost if disputed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transfer states without defined pressure-boundary acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Transfer states without pressure-boundary acceptance criteria is a criteria-completeness gap on the cascade (relevance moderate); undefined states can pass unsafe conditions, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful percentage of transfer states routinely lack defined acceptance criteria. Covering all transfer states with criteria adds modest documentation time. Comprehensive criteria coverage requires methodical protocol authoring skill. Undefined states trigger costly protocol deviations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required room-pressure and airflow inputs absent from the chamber integration evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Missing room-pressure/airflow inputs in the integration evaluation is a design-completeness error affecting cascade integration (relevance moderate); omissions can lead to an incorrectly integrated cascade, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Required inputs are commonly missing or late at chamber evaluation, a recurring completeness gap. Gathering room-pressure and airflow inputs can stall the evaluation while awaiting data. Knowing which inputs are needed requires integration expertise not always present. Missing inputs force reanalysis, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of room pressure-cascade recovery after each transfer
rows.imp_rat: Room pressure-cascade recovery time after each transfer is a throughput/readiness concern (relevance moderate); slow recovery delays next use, low-moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery reliably occurs but its duration is often longer than desired, constraining transfer rate. Cascade recovery time after each transfer directly limits throughput and is a persistent operational delay. Reducing recovery time relies on responsive HVAC control design requiring specialist tuning. Shorter recovery demands higher airflow capacity and energy cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfer-cycle states lacking pressure-boundary acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Transfer-cycle states lacking pressure-boundary acceptance criteria leaves boundary undefined per phase (relevance moderate); undefined phases risk unnoticed breach, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer-cycle states lacking acceptance criteria are a common qualification-documentation shortfall. Assigning acceptance criteria per cycle state is a bounded documentation task. Defining per-state acceptance criteria needs qualification-writing expertise. Missing criteria cause requalification cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of chamber-to-room boundary interfaces lacking pressure-integration criteria
rows.imp_rat: Chamber-to-room interfaces lacking pressure-integration criteria leaves boundary points unspecified (relevance moderate); unspecified interfaces can leak the cascade, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Interfaces lacking pressure-integration criteria are a common documentation gap in practice. Listing boundary interfaces needing criteria is a bounded activity. Ensuring every interface has integration criteria requires systematic interface-management discipline. Missed interface criteria surface as costly field changes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of expected room operating conditions outside the scope of the integration criteria
rows.imp_rat: Room operating conditions outside the integration criteria scope leaves real operating envelope unassessed (relevance moderate); out-of-scope conditions can breach unexpectedly, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Integration criteria commonly fail to cover the full range of operating conditions. Defining scope of operating conditions is a bounded planning task. Identifying all expected operating conditions needs operational and design knowledge combined. Out-of-scope conditions found later drive rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of pressure-boundary criteria lacking measurable acceptance limits
rows.imp_rat: Pressure-boundary criteria lacking measurable acceptance limits makes verification impossible (relevance moderate); unmeasurable criteria can't confirm the cascade, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria without measurable limits are a common weakness that undermines verification consistency. Attaching measurable limits to criteria is a fast editorial step. Writing measurable acceptance limits needs metrological clarity that specs often lack. Non-measurable criteria cause dispute and retest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of pressure-integration criteria lacking a verifiable acceptance condition
rows.imp_rat: Pressure-integration criteria lacking a verifiable acceptance condition makes verification impossible (relevance moderate); unverifiable criteria can't confirm cascade integration, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-integration criteria lacking verifiable conditions recur across projects. Making criteria verifiable is a bounded documentation refinement. Ensuring each criterion is verifiable requires validation expertise. Unverifiable criteria drive protocol rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between protocol parameter limits and the intended chamber operating envelope
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between protocol parameter limits and the intended operating envelope leaves envelope portions unqualified (relevance moderate); off-envelope operation may be unvalidated, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Envelope definitions are usually documented but drift between protocol limits and real operation still occurs. Reconciling parameter limits with operating envelope is upfront work, not time-driven. Defining the operating envelope correctly needs engineering and process expertise that is generally available. Mismatched limits cause requalification cost, a downstream consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of relevant chamber locations and cycle phases omitted from the sampling plan
rows.imp_rat: Chamber locations/cycle phases omitted from the sampling plan leaves contamination undetected (relevance moderate); missed sampling hides real burden, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling plans routinely omit relevant locations or phases, undermining representativeness of results. Sampling-plan design is a planning activity with no acute time constraint. Choosing representative locations and cycle phases needs statistical and process expertise that is not always present. Under-sampling forces repeat runs and added analysis cost, downstream of the coverage gap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in attributing observed contamination ingress to the chamber boundary
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in attributing observed ingress to the chamber boundary is an investigation/diagnostic gap (relevance moderate); poor attribution delays fixing the true cause, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: There are few objective methods to isolate boundary contribution, so attribution remains uncertain and contested. Attribution investigations can extend timelines but the core difficulty is not speed. Attributing ingress specifically to the chamber boundary versus other sources requires expert investigative judgement that is scarce and error-prone. Misattribution leads to wasted corrective effort, but the primary loss is diagnostic uncertainty.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contradictions between throughput and boundary-protection requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contradictions between throughput and boundary-protection requirements is a requirements-conflict error (relevance moderate); unresolved conflicts risk throughput pressure overriding protection, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Throughput-boundary contradictions are commonly left unresolved and handled ad hoc, so this is poorly served. Resolving throughput-versus-boundary conflicts is a design negotiation, not runtime timing. Balancing throughput against containment requires experienced trade-off judgement. Resolution effort adds engineering cost but no hardware change.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of control states or transitions with conflicting definitions across specifications
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting definitions of control states/transitions across specs is a specification-consistency error (relevance moderate); conflicts can yield unsafe states, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting definitions across specifications persist frequently, leaving this need poorly served. Eliminating conflicting definitions is a documentation task, not operational timing. Detecting conflicting state/transition definitions requires meticulous specification review skill. Review labor adds cost with no hardware impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about whether the funded scope covers contamination-control, pressure-cascade, and validation requirements
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about whether funded scope covers contamination/cascade/validation is a scope-definition error upstream of operation (relevance low); it risks gaps later but is not itself a breach, moderate severity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration materials remain outside specified storage conditions during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Materials outside specified storage conditions during transfer is a material-condition/quality concern (relevance low-moderate); it degrades material rather than breaching the boundary, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer is usually brief but stalls make out-of-condition exposure duration unpredictable. This is about how long materials sit outside specified storage conditions during transfer. Minimizing exposure depends on operator promptness. Spoiled material is a cost consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of false boundary-breach indications during material and personnel transfers
rows.imp_rat: False boundary-breach indications during transfers cause unnecessary stops and erode trust in alarms (relevance moderate); false positives mainly cost throughput and can mask real alerts, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: False boundary-breach signals from poorly tuned sensors are common and erode confidence in genuine alarms. False breach indications waste response time on non-events. Distinguishing false from true indications requires operator experience. Nuisance alarms cause unnecessary production stops and cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transfer events omitted from compliance review
rows.imp_rat: Transfer events omitted from compliance review is a review-coverage/records error (relevance low-moderate); missed reviews are mainly assurance/compliance gaps, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Some transfer events escape compliance review where sampling rather than full coverage is used. Compliance review is periodic and adds review time. Ensuring no transfer is omitted requires systematic reviewer discipline. Review coverage costs QA labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of identified transfer deviations unresolved when the compliance disposition is due
rows.imp_rat: Transfer deviations unresolved at compliance disposition due date is a timeliness/records error (relevance low-moderate); late resolution is mainly compliance exposure, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Open deviations at disposition time recur, undermining timely closure consistency. Deviations sometimes remain unresolved when disposition deadlines arrive, indicating a timeliness problem. Resolving deviations requires investigative expertise that is often a bottleneck. Investigation effort is a labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transfer events lacking evidence that participating personnel were qualified and authorized
rows.imp_rat: Lacking evidence of personnel qualification is largely a documentation/compliance gap, peripheral to physical boundary control (relevance low); consequence is audit exposure rather than direct contamination, moderate severity.
